1→# MMS - Metro Mileage Management System
     2→
     3→> Enterprise solution to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets (>100 units), bridging Operations and Maintenance via predictive, data-driven planning integrated with EAM.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Overview
     8→
     9→MMS replaces manual workflows with a rule-based, predictive engine that:
    10→- Optimizes preventative maintenance scheduling
    11→- Balances mileage across the fleet
    12→- Provides unified interface between Maintenance and Operations teams
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Domain Glossary
    17→
    18→| Term                    | Definition                                                                                                                     | Type        |
    19→|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
    20→| **Rolling Stock**       | Railway vehicles (trains, carriages) requiring maintenance                                                                     | Entity      |
    21→| **Fleet**               | Group of rolling stock sharing same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile                                                 | Entity      |
    22→| **Maintenance Profile** | Set of PM intervals, overhaul schedules, and resource requirements specific to a fleet type                                    | Entity      |
    23→| **Base Depot**          | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet                                                                                    | Entity      |
    24→| **Train Fitness**       | Binary operational readiness (fit/unfit) determined by Maintenance team or EAM. Unfit trains are excluded from run assignments | State       |
    25→| **Run Assignment**      | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/network based on fitness and mileage targets                                           | Action      |
    26→| **Mileage Balancing**   | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet, preventing underrun/overrun                                                            | Goal        |
    27→| **EAM**                 | Enterprise Asset Management - external system for asset lifecycle (e.g., IBM Maximo, SAP PM)                                   | Integration |
    28→| **TCO**                 | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle                                                                                   | Metric      |
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## System Inputs
    33→
    34→| Category        | Input                     | Description                                |
    35→|-----------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
    36→| **Fleet**       | Train IDs                 | Unique identifier per train                |
    37→|                 | Fleet Type                | Classification/manufacturer grouping       |
    38→|                 | Base Depot                | Designated maintenance depot per fleet     |
    39→| **Mileage**     | Daily Mileage             | Real-time km per train (live data)         |
    40→| **Operations**  | Train Fitness Status      | Daily fitness state per train              |
    41→|                 | Operation Requirements    | Daily demand per train run ID              |
    42→| **Maintenance** | Maintenance Plans         | PM schedules, overhaul intervals           |
    43→|                 | Track Availability        | Maintenance track slots                    |
    44→|                 | Man-hours per Maintenance | Labor hours by maintenance type            |
    45→| **Costs**       | Material Cost             | Spare parts cost per maintenance type      |
    46→|                 | Service Cost              | External service cost per maintenance type |
    47→|                 | Man-hour Cost             | Labor rate                                 |
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→## System Outputs
    52→
    53→| Output                   | Description                                                                | Format         |
    54→|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------|
    55→| **Dashboard**            | Fleet-wide mileage status, high-level KPIs                                 | Web UI         |
    56→| **Maintenance Status**   | Current mileage position per train relative to next PM/overhaul            | Web UI         |
    57→| **Run Assignments**      | Daily train allocation recommendations based on fitness + mileage balance  | Web UI, Export |
    58→| **Maintenance Schedule** | Completed and upcoming PM/overhaul activities                              | Web UI, Export |
    59→| **Compliance Status**    | Real-time adherence to maintenance requirements                            | Web UI         |
    60→| **Cost Compliance**      | Actual vs. Planned maintenance costs (monthly/yearly)                      | Web UI, Export |
    61→| **Budget Forecast**      | Projected maintenance costs per fleet over planning horizon                | Web UI, Export |
    62→| **Simulation Reports**   | Scenario comparison: cost impact, compliance risk, fleet wear distribution | PDF, Excel     |
    63→| **Standard Reports**     | Configurable reports for stakeholders                                      | PDF, Excel     |
    64→| **Notifications**        | Alerts via in-app and email                                                | Push, Email    |
    65→
    66→---
    67→
    68→## Core Modules
    69→
    70→### 1. Maintenance Planning
    71→
    72→Generates optimized schedules from short-term to long-term horizons.
    73→
    74→| Horizon    | Scope                     | Objective                              |
    75→|------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------|
    76→| Short-term | Weekly, monthly schedules | Execution feasibility, resource lock   |
    77→| Long-term  | 1-10 year strategic plans | Budget forecasting, lifecycle strategy |
    78→
    79→**Constraints:**
    80→- Man-hour availability
    81→- Track allocation
    82→- Train availability
    83→
    84→**Outputs:**
    85→- Optimized maintenance schedules
    86→- Warnings for mileage underrun/overrun risks
    87→- Scenario simulation capabilities
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→### 2. Operation Planning
    92→
    93→Dynamic assignment engine for daily fleet deployment.
    94→
    95→**Flow:**
    96→1. **Input:** Daily operation requirements (train run IDs, network demand)
    97→2. **Process:** Filters available trains by fitness → Ranks by mileage delta (target vs actual)
    98→3. **Output:** `TrainID` + network/route assignment recommendation
    99→4. **Loop:** Ingests actual daily km to update predictions and trigger corrections
   100→
   101→**Interface:** Unified Maintenance ↔ Ops communication for train fitness and mileage management.
   102→
   103→---
   104→
   105→### 3. Resource Planning
   106→
   107→Strategic module for maintenance costing and resource optimization.
   108→
   109→**Capabilities:**
   110→- Resource smoothing: distributes man-hours and spare parts evenly
   111→- Prevents resource peaks/troughs over planning horizon
   112→- Maintenance costing per fleet based on mileage and plan
   113→
   114→**Fixed Parameters:**
   115→- Manpower availability
   116→- Spare parts costs (material + service)
   117→- Track capacity
   118→
   119→---
   120→
   121→## Delivery Phases
   122→
   123→| Phase       | Scope                                                                                 | Status |
   124→|-------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------|
   125→| **Phase 1** | Mileage management, run assignments                                                   | -      |
   126→| **Phase 2** | Financial modeling (costing per fleet, budget compliance), EAM integration capability | -      |
   127→
   128→---
   129→
   130→## Architecture & Data Flow
   131→
   132→```
   133→[Operations] <--(Interface/Comms)--> [Maintenance]
   134→      |                                    |
   135→      | (Actuals)                          | (Plans)
   136→      v                                    v
   137→+-----------------------------------------------------+
   138→|                        MMS                          |
   139→|  +-----------+-----------+-----------+              |
   140→|  |Maintenance| Operation | Resource  |              |
   141→|  | Planning  | Planning  | Planning  |              |
   142→|  +-----------+-----------+-----------+              |
   143→+--------------------+--------------------------------+
   144→                     | (Work Orders / Asset Data)
   145→                     v
   146→              +-------------+
   147→              |     EAM     |
   148→              |(Integration)|
   149→              +-------------+
   150→```
   151→
   152→---
   153→
   154→## User Roles
   155→
   156→| Role            | Scope                            | Permissions                                                      | Restrictions                                    |
   157→|-----------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
   158→| **Admin**       | System-wide                      | Full configuration, user management, all data access             | None                                            |
   159→| **Maintenance** | Planning, scheduling, resources  | Edit maintenance plans, track allocation, cost data, set fitness | Cannot edit run assignments or mileage actuals  |
   160→| **Operations**  | Run assignments, daily execution | Edit daily assignments, input mileage actuals                    | Cannot edit maintenance plans or fitness status |
   161→
   162→---
   163→
   164→## For AI Agents
   165→
   166→**System purpose:** Optimize rolling stock maintenance scheduling and mileage distribution.
   167→
   168→**Optimization objective:** Minimize mileage variance across fleet while respecting maintenance windows and resource constraints.
   169→
   170→### Entity Model
   171→
   172→```
   173→Fleet (1) ---- (*) Train
   174→  |                 |
   175→  |                 +---- (*) RunAssignment
   176→  |                 |
   177→  |                 +---- (1) TrainFitness [daily state, set by Maintenance]
   178→  |
   179→  +---- (1) BaseDepot
   180→  |
   181→  +---- (1) MaintenanceProfile
   182→
   183→MaintenancePlan (1) ---- (*) MaintenanceActivity
   184→       |
   185→       +---- constrains ---- RunAssignment
   186→
   187→Cost
   188→  +-- MaterialCost [per maintenance type]
   189→  +-- ServiceCost [per maintenance type]
   190→  +-- ManHourCost [rate]
   191→```
   192→
   193→### Optional Integrations
   194→
   195→```
   196→EAM (external)
   197→  +-- syncs ---- AssetData [master]
   198→  +-- syncs ---- WorkOrders
   199→```
   200→
   201→### Fixed Parameters (Constraints)
   202→
   203→- Man-hour limits (per depot, per period)
   204→- Track availability (slots per depot)
   205→- Cost baselines (material, service, labor)
   206→
   207→### Configurable Thresholds
   208→
   209→| Parameter                | Default | Description                  |
   210→|--------------------------|---------|------------------------------|
   211→| `mileage_underrun_pct`   | -10%    | Trigger warning below target |
   212→| `mileage_overrun_pct`    | +10%    | Trigger warning above target |
   213→| `planning_horizon_years` | 10      | Long-term planning window    |
   214→
   215→### Business Rules
   216→
   217→- `IF` predicted ≠ actual mileage `THEN` trigger warning + recommend adjustment
   218→- `IF` train approaching underrun/overrun threshold `THEN` trigger warning + corrective guidance
   219→- `IF` train unfit `THEN` exclude from run assignment candidates
   220→- Resource planning must avoid peaks/troughs over planning horizon
   221→
   222→### Constraint Priority (highest to lowest)
   223→
   224→1. **Train Fitness** - Unfit trains never assigned
   225→2. **Maintenance Windows** - Scheduled maintenance blocks availability
   226→3. **Resource Limits** - Man-hours, track slots per depot
   227→4. **Mileage Balance** - Optimize within above constraints
   228→
   229→### Integration Points
   230→
   231→- **Input:** Daily mileage (live), fitness status, operation requirements
   232→- **Output:** Optimized schedules, run assignments, compliance reports
   233→- **External (optional):** EAM sync - work orders, asset master data
   234→
   235→---
   236→
   237→## Platform Capabilities
   238→
   239→**Interface:**
   240→- Web-based application (desktop + mobile)
   241→- Centralized dashboard with fleet-wide visibility
   242→- Customizable alerts/notifications (in-app + email)
   243→
   244→**Technical:**
   245→- Scalable architecture for additional fleets
   246→- Data security and IT compliance
   247→- EAM integration capability (API-based)
   248→
   249→**Reports:**
   250→- Standard and custom report generation (PDF, Excel)
   251→- Simulation reports for different mileage management strategies
   252→- Email notification of generated reports to stakeholders

file.content:
# MMS - Metro Mileage Management System

> Enterprise solution to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets (>100 units), bridging Operations and Maintenance via predictive, data-driven planning integrated with EAM.

---

## Overview

MMS replaces manual workflows with a rule-based, predictive engine that:
- Optimizes preventative maintenance scheduling
- Balances mileage across the fleet
- Provides unified interface between Maintenance and Operations teams

---

## Domain Glossary

| Term                    | Definition                                                                                                                     | Type        |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| **Rolling Stock**       | Railway vehicles (trains, carriages) requiring maintenance                                                                     | Entity      |
| **Fleet**               | Group of rolling stock sharing same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile                                                 | Entity      |
| **Maintenance Profile** | Set of PM intervals, overhaul schedules, and resource requirements specific to a fleet type                                    | Entity      |
| **Base Depot**          | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet                                                                                    | Entity      |
| **Train Fitness**       | Binary operational readiness (fit/unfit) determined by Maintenance team or EAM. Unfit trains are excluded from run assignments | State       |
| **Run Assignment**      | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/network based on fitness and mileage targets                                           | Action      |
| **Mileage Balancing**   | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet, preventing underrun/overrun                                                            | Goal        |
| **EAM**                 | Enterprise Asset Management - external system for asset lifecycle (e.g., IBM Maximo, SAP PM)                                   | Integration |
| **TCO**                 | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle                                                                                   | Metric      |

---

## System Inputs

| Category        | Input                     | Description                                |
|-----------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| **Fleet**       | Train IDs                 | Unique identifier per train                |
|                 | Fleet Type                | Classification/manufacturer grouping       |
|                 | Base Depot                | Designated maintenance depot per fleet     |
| **Mileage**     | Daily Mileage             | Real-time km per train (live data)         |
| **Operations**  | Train Fitness Status      | Daily fitness state per train              |
|                 | Operation Requirements    | Daily demand per train run ID              |
| **Maintenance** | Maintenance Plans         | PM schedules, overhaul intervals           |
|                 | Track Availability        | Maintenance track slots                    |
|                 | Man-hours per Maintenance | Labor hours by maintenance type            |
| **Costs**       | Material Cost             | Spare parts cost per maintenance type      |
|                 | Service Cost              | External service cost per maintenance type |
|                 | Man-hour Cost             | Labor rate                                 |

---

## System Outputs

| Output                   | Description                                                                | Format         |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------|
| **Dashboard**            | Fleet-wide mileage status, high-level KPIs                                 | Web UI         |
| **Maintenance Status**   | Current mileage position per train relative to next PM/overhaul            | Web UI         |
| **Run Assignments**      | Daily train allocation recommendations based on fitness + mileage balance  | Web UI, Export |
| **Maintenance Schedule** | Completed and upcoming PM/overhaul activities                              | Web UI, Export |
| **Compliance Status**    | Real-time adherence to maintenance requirements                            | Web UI         |
| **Cost Compliance**      | Actual vs. Planned maintenance costs (monthly/yearly)                      | Web UI, Export |
| **Budget Forecast**      | Projected maintenance costs per fleet over planning horizon                | Web UI, Export |
| **Simulation Reports**   | Scenario comparison: cost impact, compliance risk, fleet wear distribution | PDF, Excel     |
| **Standard Reports**     | Configurable reports for stakeholders                                      | PDF, Excel     |
| **Notifications**        | Alerts via in-app and email                                                | Push, Email    |

---

## Core Modules

### 1. Maintenance Planning

Generates optimized schedules from short-term to long-term horizons.

| Horizon    | Scope                     | Objective                              |
|------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Short-term | Weekly, monthly schedules | Execution feasibility, resource lock   |
| Long-term  | 1-10 year strategic plans | Budget forecasting, lifecycle strategy |

**Constraints:**
- Man-hour availability
- Track allocation
- Train availability

**Outputs:**
- Optimized maintenance schedules
- Warnings for mileage underrun/overrun risks
- Scenario simulation capabilities

---

### 2. Operation Planning

Dynamic assignment engine for daily fleet deployment.

**Flow:**
1. **Input:** Daily operation requirements (train run IDs, network demand)
2. **Process:** Filters available trains by fitness → Ranks by mileage delta (target vs actual)
3. **Output:** `TrainID` + network/route assignment recommendation
4. **Loop:** Ingests actual daily km to update predictions and trigger corrections

**Interface:** Unified Maintenance ↔ Ops communication for train fitness and mileage management.

---

### 3. Resource Planning

Strategic module for maintenance costing and resource optimization.

**Capabilities:**
- Resource smoothing: distributes man-hours and spare parts evenly
- Prevents resource peaks/troughs over planning horizon
- Maintenance costing per fleet based on mileage and plan

**Fixed Parameters:**
- Manpower availability
- Spare parts costs (material + service)
- Track capacity

---

## Delivery Phases

| Phase       | Scope                                                                                 | Status |
|-------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------|
| **Phase 1** | Mileage management, run assignments                                                   | -      |
| **Phase 2** | Financial modeling (costing per fleet, budget compliance), EAM integration capability | -      |

---

## Architecture & Data Flow

```
[Operations] <--(Interface/Comms)--> [Maintenance]
      |                                    |
      | (Actuals)                          | (Plans)
      v                                    v
+-----------------------------------------------------+
|                        MMS                          |
|  +-----------+-----------+-----------+              |
|  |Maintenance| Operation | Resource  |              |
|  | Planning  | Planning  | Planning  |              |
|  +-----------+-----------+-----------+              |
+--------------------+--------------------------------+
                     | (Work Orders / Asset Data)
                     v
              +-------------+
              |     EAM     |
              |(Integration)|
              +-------------+
```

---

## User Roles

| Role            | Scope                            | Permissions                                                      | Restrictions                                    |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| **Admin**       | System-wide                      | Full configuration, user management, all data access             | None                                            |
| **Maintenance** | Planning, scheduling, resources  | Edit maintenance plans, track allocation, cost data, set fitness | Cannot edit run assignments or mileage actuals  |
| **Operations**  | Run assignments, daily execution | Edit daily assignments, input mileage actuals                    | Cannot edit maintenance plans or fitness status |

---

## For AI Agents

**System purpose:** Optimize rolling stock maintenance scheduling and mileage distribution.

**Optimization objective:** Minimize mileage variance across fleet while respecting maintenance windows and resource constraints.

### Entity Model

```
Fleet (1) ---- (*) Train
  |                 |
  |                 +---- (*) RunAssignment
  |                 |
  |                 +---- (1) TrainFitness [daily state, set by Maintenance]
  |
  +---- (1) BaseDepot
  |
  +---- (1) MaintenanceProfile

MaintenancePlan (1) ---- (*) MaintenanceActivity
       |
       +---- constrains ---- RunAssignment

Cost
  +-- MaterialCost [per maintenance type]
  +-- ServiceCost [per maintenance type]
  +-- ManHourCost [rate]
```

### Optional Integrations

```
EAM (external)
  +-- syncs ---- AssetData [master]
  +-- syncs ---- WorkOrders
```

### Fixed Parameters (Constraints)

- Man-hour limits (per depot, per period)
- Track availability (slots per depot)
- Cost baselines (material, service, labor)

### Configurable Thresholds

| Parameter                | Default | Description                  |
|--------------------------|---------|------------------------------|
| `mileage_underrun_pct`   | -10%    | Trigger warning below target |
| `mileage_overrun_pct`    | +10%    | Trigger warning above target |
| `planning_horizon_years` | 10      | Long-term planning window    |

### Business Rules

- `IF` predicted ≠ actual mileage `THEN` trigger warning + recommend adjustment
- `IF` train approaching underrun/overrun threshold `THEN` trigger warning + corrective guidance
- `IF` train unfit `THEN` exclude from run assignment candidates
- Resource planning must avoid peaks/troughs over planning horizon

### Constraint Priority (highest to lowest)

1. **Train Fitness** - Unfit trains never assigned
2. **Maintenance Windows** - Scheduled maintenance blocks availability
3. **Resource Limits** - Man-hours, track slots per depot
4. **Mileage Balance** - Optimize within above constraints

### Integration Points

- **Input:** Daily mileage (live), fitness status, operation requirements
- **Output:** Optimized schedules, run assignments, compliance reports
- **External (optional):** EAM sync - work orders, asset master data

---

## Platform Capabilities

**Interface:**
- Web-based application (desktop + mobile)
- Centralized dashboard with fleet-wide visibility
- Customizable alerts/notifications (in-app + email)

**Technical:**
- Scalable architecture for additional fleets
- Data security and IT compliance
- EAM integration capability (API-based)

**Reports:**
- Standard and custom report generation (PDF, Excel)
- Simulation reports for different mileage management strategies
- Email notification of generated reports to stakeholders

content.content: PDF file read: /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms/MFC/docs/RFQ.pdf (278.3KB)

1→# Railway Rolling Stock Maintenance Scheduling: Research & Industry Practices
     2→
     3→## Table of Contents
     4→
     5→1. [Executive Summary](#executive-summary)
     6→2. [Problem Definition](#problem-definition)
     7→3. [Planning Hierarchy](#planning-hierarchy)
     8→4. [Optimization Techniques](#optimization-techniques)
     9→5. [Physical Constraints Modeling](#physical-constraints-modeling)
    10→6. [Case Studies by Metro System](#case-studies-by-metro-system)
    11→7. [Technology & Systems](#technology--systems)
    12→8. [Data Architecture](#data-architecture)
    13→9. [Academic Research](#academic-research)
    14→10. [Best Practices](#best-practices)
    15→11. [Risk Management & Edge Cases](#risk-management--edge-cases)
    16→12. [Human-in-the-Loop Integration](#human-in-the-loop-integration)
    17→13. [Change Propagation & Recalculation](#change-propagation--recalculation-mechanics)
    18→14. [Future Directions](#future-directions)
    19→15. [References](#references)
    20→16. [Glossary](#appendix-glossary)
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## Executive Summary
    25→
    26→Railway rolling stock maintenance scheduling is a complex optimization problem that combines:
    27→- **Resource allocation** (man-hours, tracks, depots)
    28→- **Time/mileage constraints** (preventive maintenance intervals)
    29→- **Operational requirements** (train availability, service levels)
    30→- **Cost optimization** (labor, materials, penalties)
    31→
    32→Key findings from our research:
    33→
    34→| Aspect             | Industry Standard                                                 |
    35→|--------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
    36→| Planning horizons  | Daily → Weekly → Monthly → Annual → Long-term (10+ years)         |
    37→| Primary trigger    | Mileage-based (evolving to condition-based)                       |
    38→| Common techniques  | MIP, Genetic Algorithms, Large Neighborhood Search, Hybrid CP/MIP |
    39→| Software platforms | IBM Maximo, SAP EAM, Custom optimization engines                  |
    40→| Performance target | 99.9% train availability (Hong Kong MTR benchmark)                |
    41→
    42→---
    43→
    44→## Problem Definition
    45→
    46→### The Core Scheduling Problem
    47→
    48→The railway maintenance scheduling problem involves:
    49→
    50→1. **Fleet Management**: Multiple train types with different maintenance requirements
    51→2. **Maintenance Types**: Varying intervals and durations
    52→   - Daily inspections
    53→   - Monthly checks (~15,000 km)
    54→   - Quarterly services (~45,000 km)
    55→   - Annual overhauls (~180,000 km)
    56→   - Major overhauls (4-6 years)
    57→
    58→3. **Resource Constraints**:
    59→   - Limited maintenance tracks
    60→   - Fixed workforce (man-hours per period)
    61→   - Depot capacity
    62→   - Spare parts availability
    63→
    64→4. **Objectives** (often conflicting):
    65→   - Minimize maintenance costs
    66→   - Maximize train availability
    67→   - Level resource utilization
    68→   - Comply with safety regulations
    69→
    70→### Problem Complexity
    71→
    72→According to research cited in [Wiley - Optimization of Annual Planned Rail Maintenance](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764) (original source: Khalouli et al., IEEE CoDIT 2016):
    73→
    74→> "Using the CPLEX MIP solver, researchers managed to solve only 62% of instances within 3 hours. Kiefer et al. (2018) formulated their problem as an MIP model but quickly found runtime limitations on larger instances, exceeding the 24-hour time limit."
    75→
    76→This demonstrates why heuristic and metaheuristic approaches are essential for real-world applications.
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Planning Hierarchy
    81→
    82→### Multi-Level Planning Structure
    83→
    84→Based on [JICA Urban Railways Handbook](https://www.jica.go.jp/english/activities/issues/transport/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2025/03/26/handbook_en_01.pdf) and industry practices:
    85→
    86→```
    87→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    88→│                     MASTER PLAN (Annual/Multi-Year)             │
    89→│  - Resource allocation across the contract period               │
    90→│  - Major overhaul scheduling                                    │
    91→│  - Budget planning                                              │
    92→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    93→│                     MONTHLY PLAN                                │
    94→│  - Medium-scale repairs allocation                              │
    95→│  - Workload balancing                                           │
    96→│  - Resource adjustment                                          │
    97→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    98→│                     WEEKLY PLAN                                 │
    99→│  - Detailed work orders                                         │
   100→│  - Staff rostering                                              │
   101→│  - Track allocation                                             │
   102→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   103→│                     DAILY PLAN                                  │
   104→│  - Execution scheduling                                         │
   105→│  - Real-time adjustments                                        │
   106→│  - Emergency response                                           │
   107→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   108→```
   109→
   110→### Tokyo Metro Approach
   111→
   112→From [JICA Rolling Stock Maintenance Manuals](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12251708_02.pdf):
   113→
   114→> "Tokyo Metro employs an 'Equalizing' approach to secure the required work progress, equalize the maintenance workload, and enable rolling stock maintenance personnel to fulfill their duties."
   115→
   116→Key principles:
   117→- **Even workload distribution** across periods
   118→- **Mileage tracking** for devices requiring km-based maintenance
   119→- **Train allocation** at daily, monthly, and annual levels

   122→
   123→## Optimization Techniques
   124→
   125→### 1. Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)
   126→
   127→**Strengths:**
   128→- Mathematically optimal solutions
   129→- Well-understood theory
   130→- Commercial solvers available (Gurobi, CPLEX)
   131→
   132→**Limitations:**
   133→- Scalability issues with large instances
   134→- Long computation times
   135→- Requires linearization of constraints
   136→
   137→**Example Application - Deutsche Bahn:**
   138→
   139→From [Deutsche Bahn Hypergraph Optimization Case Study](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/inte.2020.1069):
   140→
   141→> "DB attributes annual savings of €74 million, an annual reduction of 34,000 tons of CO2 emissions, and the elimination of 600 coupling operations to the implementation of FEO (Fleet Employment Optimization)."
   142→
   143→### 2. Genetic Algorithms (GA)
   144→
   145→**Strengths:**
   146→- Handles complex, non-linear constraints
   147→- Good for multi-objective optimization
   148→- Scalable to large problems
   149→
   150→**Example Application - Hong Kong MTR:**
   151→
   152→From [CityU AI Projects - MTR Scheduling](https://www.cs.cityu.edu.hk/~hwchun/AIProjects/stories/allocation/mtrcscheduling/):
   153→
   154→> "The scheduling system is based on genetic algorithm and heuristic search, which work together with a rule-based system. The AI system improved maintenance efficiency by 50%, contributing to MTR's 99.9% on-time performance."
   155→
   156→*Note: MTR serves 5.18 million daily passengers as of 2024.*
   157→
   158→### 3. Large Neighborhood Search (LNS)
   159→
   160→**Strengths:**
   161→- Effective for large-scale problems
   162→- Combines local search with diversification
   163→- Can escape local optima
   164→
   165→**Example Application - Vienna Tram:**
   166→
   167→From [ScienceDirect - Integrated Railway Planning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340):
   168→
   169→> "Kiefer et al. (2018) developed a metaheuristic based on large neighborhood search after MIP exceeded 24-hour time limits. Testing on the Vienna tram network—the largest instance in literature—showed effective results."
   170→
   171→### 4. Hybrid Constraint Programming / MIP
   172→
   173→**Strengths:**
   174→- CP generates feasible solutions quickly
   175→- MIP improves solution quality
   176→- Best of both worlds
   177→
   178→**Example Application - Danish Railways:**
   179→
   180→From [ScienceDirect - Hybrid CP/MIP Framework](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0377221717307646):
   181→
   182→> "Whereas using a general purpose MIP solver is only able to solve instances over a two-week planning horizon, the hybrid framework generates good results for problem instances over an eight-week period."
   183→
   184→### 5. Greedy Metaheuristics
   185→
   186→**Strengths:**
   187→- Fast computation
   188→- Good initial solutions
   189→- Easy to implement
   190→
   191→**Example Application - Netherlands ProRail:**
   192→
   193→From [Wiley - Annual Rail Maintenance Optimization](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764):
   194→
   195→> "A hybrid algorithm using evolution strategy and a greedy algorithm was created and shown to outperform all other algorithms, achieving a two-thirds decrease in costs compared to human scheduler solutions."
   196→
   197→### Technique Comparison Matrix
   198→
   199→| Technique                 | Problem Size | Solution Quality | Speed     | Implementation |
   200→|---------------------------|--------------|------------------|-----------|----------------|
   201→| MIP                       | Small-Medium | Optimal          | Slow      | Complex        |
   202→| Genetic Algorithm         | Large        | Good             | Medium    | Moderate       |
   203→| Large Neighborhood Search | Large        | Good             | Fast      | Moderate       |
   204→| Hybrid CP/MIP             | Medium-Large | Very Good        | Medium    | Complex        |
   205→| Greedy Heuristics         | Any          | Acceptable       | Very Fast | Simple         |
   206→
   207→### Algorithm Selection Strategy for MMS
   208→
   209→Based on the research, a layered algorithmic architecture is recommended:
   210→
   211→1. **Strategic/Long-Term (Annual):** MIP for capacity planning and yearly workload leveling
   212→2. **Tactical/Medium-Term (Weeks):** CP or MIP with decomposition (LBBD) for slot assignment respecting depot constraints
   213→3. **Operational/Real-Time (Hours):** Heuristics and Metaheuristics for fast rescheduling during disruptions
   214→
   215→---
   216→
   217→## Physical Constraints Modeling
   218→
   219→A common failure in traditional planning systems is over-abstraction of physical reality. The MMS must rigorously model depot and rolling stock constraints to ensure generated plans are executable.
   220→
   221→### Pit Occupancy and Finite Capacity
   222→
   223→Depot capacity is not just a track count; it's a complex resource and geometry constraint problem.
   224→
   225→**The "Tetris" Spatial Problem:**
   226→Trains have different lengths (4-car, 8-car). Pits have fixed lengths. A "Pit Occupancy" constraint must model spatial arrangement:
   227→- Can two 4-car trains fit in an 8-car pit?
   228→- What order must they enter (LIFO - Last-In-First-Out)?
   229→
   230→This requires modeling tracks not just as "resources" but as "capacitated resources" with linear spatial dimensions.
   231→
   232→**Finite Capacity Modeling:**
   233→```
   234→∑ Occupancy_t ≤ Capacity_t  (for all resource types simultaneously)
   235→```
   236→
   237→**Deadlock Prevention:**
   238→In dense depots, one train's movement can block another. "Deadlocks" occur when circular movement dependencies prevent any train from moving.
   239→
   240→The MMS must incorporate deadlock prevention algorithms (such as adaptations of the Banker's Algorithm or graph cycle detection) during shunting planning to ensure every scheduled movement sequence is geometrically viable.
   241→
   242→### Shunting Time and Dynamics
   243→
   244→The time to move a train from parking track to maintenance pit is non-zero and variable.
   245→
   246→**Train Unit Shunting Problem (TUSP):**
   247→This involves matching arriving train units with departing services and parking them in LIFO stacks on depot tracks. Stack order is critical; a train departing at 05:00 cannot be blocked by one leaving at 08:00.
   248→
   249→**Crossover Conflicts:**
   250→Modeling must account for conflicts where a shunting movement crosses a switch that blocks other movements. Constraints must impose temporal separation:
   251→```
   252→If Train A uses Switch S at time t,
   253→Train B cannot use Switch S until t + clearance_time
   254→```
   255→
   256→**Shunting Duration:**
   257→The system cannot assume instantaneous movement. Shunting times must be modeled based on track topology and speed limits (typically 5 km/h in yards).
   258→
   259→### Possession Windows and Infrastructure Availability
   260→
   261→Rolling stock maintenance often competes with infrastructure maintenance (permanent way, signaling, catenary).
   262→
   263→**Possession Windows:**
   264→Constraints must align rolling stock movements with possession windows—periods when tracks are closed for maintenance. This requires a unified data model where infrastructure availability acts as a hard constraint on fleet movement.
   265→

   507→
   508→## Data Architecture
   509→
   510→The complexity of an MMS requires a data architecture that can handle high-frequency sensor data (Digital Twin), complex transactional planning (Scheduling), and historical analysis (Reporting/BI). Research points to the superiority of the CQRS pattern over traditional CRUD architectures.
   511→
   512→### CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)
   513→
   514→Traditional CRUD architectures struggle when read and write patterns are vastly different, as in an MMS.
   515→
   516→**Write Model (Command):**
   517→The "Command" side handles the complex business logic of scheduling—constraint validation, state updates, and user override processing. This requires a highly normalized transactional database (OLTP) to ensure data integrity and ACID properties.
   518→
   519→**Read Model (Query):**
   520→The "Query" side is optimized for visualization. Planners need to see Gantt charts, depot occupancy heatmaps, and fleet status summaries instantly. CQRS allows the creation of denormalized "projections" specifically designed for these views, enabling fast load times without complex joins.
   521→
   522→**Strategic Benefit:**
   523→This separation allows the heavy computational load of optimization algorithms (running on the Write side) not to impact the responsiveness of the user interface (reading from the Read side).
   524→
   525→### Event Sourcing as Source of Truth
   526→
   527→Event Sourcing complements CQRS by storing *state changes* (events) rather than just current state.
   528→
   529→**Auditability and "Time Travel":**
   530→In a safety-critical industry like rail, knowing *who* changed a schedule and *why* is crucial. Event Sourcing stores an immutable log of events:
   531→- `MaintenanceScheduled`
   532→- `TrainDelayed`
   533→- `InspectionCompleted`
   534→- `ConstraintOverridden`
   535→
   536→This allows the system to reconstruct schedule state at any point in the past, facilitating post-incident analysis and "what-if" simulations.
   537→
   538→**Digital Twin Integration:**
   539→IoT sensor events (e.g., `WheelWearThresholdExceeded`) can be ingested directly as events into the log, triggering downstream reactions in scheduling logic.
   540→
   541→### Transactional vs. Analytical Data Stores
   542→
   543→| Aspect        | Transactional (OLTP)                | Analytical (OLAP)                                 |
   544→|---------------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
   545→| Purpose       | Main scheduling engine              | BI and reporting                                  |
   546→| Consistency   | Immediate consistency               | Eventual consistency                              |
   547→| Query Pattern | Single-record lookups               | Complex aggregations                              |
   548→| Tool Example  | PostgreSQL, SQL Server              | Apache Superset, Data Warehouse                   |
   549→| MMS Use       | Plan generation, real-time dispatch | "Average pit occupancy last year", trend analysis |
   550→
   551→BI tools like Apache Superset should connect to a separate Data Warehouse (fed by Read Model projections) without blocking the operational database used by real-time dispatchers.
   552→

   612→## Best Practices
   613→
   614→### 1. Maintenance Trigger Selection
   615→
   616→| Trigger Type    | Best For             | Limitations               |
   617→|-----------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
   618→| Time-based      | Simple assets        | Over/under maintenance    |
   619→| Mileage-based   | Wear-dependent items | Tracking complexity       |
   620→| Condition-based | Critical components  | Sensor investment         |
   621→| Hybrid          | Most applications    | Implementation complexity |
   622→
   623→From [Railway Technical Website](http://www.railway-technical.com/trains/train-maintenance/):
   624→
   625→> "Rolling stock maintenance can be programmed by mileage, by time, or by condition monitoring. Condition monitoring is most recent and reliable, relying on automated data collection and sensors."
   626→
   627→### 2. Resource Leveling
   628→
   629→**Goal**: Even distribution of workload to avoid peaks and valleys
   630→
   631→**Techniques**:
   632→- Move flexible events within tolerance windows
   633→- Balance by fleet type
   634→- Consider depot-specific constraints
   635→- Account for seasonal variations
   636→
   637→From Tokyo Metro practice:
   638→
   639→> "Allocating an even workload in the annual maintenance plan provides advantages for both resource utilization and service reliability."
   640→
   641→### 3. Mileage Equalization
   642→
   643→**Problem**: Uneven mileage leads to clustered maintenance
   644→
   645→From [ScienceDirect - Long-term Fleet Management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2210970623000628):
   646→
   647→> "A sub-optimal choice of wagons can lead to situations requiring complex shunting operations. If wagons were clustered by mileage, the process would be greatly simplified."
   648→
   649→**Solution**: Strategic run assignment to balance fleet mileage
   650→
   651→### 4. Integration with Operations
   652→
   653→**Key Principle**: Maintenance and operations must be coordinated
   654→
   655→From [KONUX and Deutsche Bahn](https://www.konux.com/konux-receives-another-significant-order-from-deutsche-bahn/):
   656→
   657→> "Real-time insights into current and future condition of infrastructure enables scheduling timely interventions such as grinding, welding, or replacement."
   658→
   659→### 5. Performance Metrics
   660→
   661→| Metric       | Definition                     | Target   |
   662→|--------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   663→| MTBF         | Mean Time Between Failures     | Maximize |
   664→| MDBF         | Mean Distance Between Failures | Maximize |
   665→| Availability | % time train is operational    | >99%     |
   666→| Utilization  | % capacity used                | 70-90%   |
   667→| Compliance   | % on-time maintenance          | >95%     |
   668→

   714→## Risk Management & Edge Cases
   715→
   716→A plan is only as good as its resilience to reality. The MMS must account for the stochastic nature of railway operations and be prepared for the unexpected.
   717→
   718→### Risk and Edge Case Catalog
   719→
   720→| Category          | Risk / Edge Case              | Description & Impact                                                                       | Software Mitigation Strategy                                                    |
   721→|:------------------|:------------------------------|:-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|:--------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
   722→| **Technological** | **Sensor Drift**              | IoT sensors report false positives or lose calibration, leading to unnecessary maintenance | Data fusion algorithms and cross-validation with history                        |
   723→| **Technological** | **Data Gaps**                 | Loss of connectivity in tunnels results in incomplete telemetry                            | Data interpolation (Kriging) and Bayesian predictive models                     |
   724→| **Operational**   | **Yard Deadlocks**            | Circular blocking where no train can move, paralyzing the depot                            | Graph cycle detection algorithms integrated into shunting module                |
   725→| **Operational**   | **Cascade Delays**            | A minor delay makes the maintenance plan infeasible                                        | Robust rescheduling with delay "absorption" metrics                             |
   726→| **Operational**   | **Infeasibility**             | Scenarios where *no* solution satisfies all hard constraints                               | Use "Soft Constraints" with penalty minimization (offer the "least bad" option) |
   727→| **Physical**      | **Component Cannibalization** | Removing parts from one train to fix another, complicating asset tracking                  | Serialized component tracking in data model (dynamic asset tree)                |
   728→| **Physical**      | **Weather Events**            | Extreme heat expanding rails or leaves on track affecting braking                          | Dynamic adjustment of shunting time parameters and speed constraints            |
   729→
   730→### Robustness and Stability Metrics
   731→
   732→**Nervousness:**
   733→A schedule is "nervous" if small input changes lead to massive plan changes. To mitigate, the MMS must employ "stability objectives" in the optimization function, penalizing alterations to already published or communicated tasks:
   734→
   735→```
   736→Minimize (Operational_Cost + λ × Instability_Cost)
   737→```
   738→
   739→**Key Robustness Metrics:**
   740→
   741→| Metric           | Definition                                                      | Target   |
   742→|------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------|----------|
   743→| Absorption       | Ability of schedule to absorb small delays without rescheduling | Maximize |
   744→| Recovery Time    | How quickly system returns to original plan after disruption    | Minimize |
   745→| Passenger Impact | Delays weighted by affected passengers                          | Minimize |
   746→
   747→### Software Risk Mitigation Strategies
   748→
   749→1. **Algorithmic Risk (Solver Timeout):** Implement an "Algorithm Portfolio". System tries MIP; if time exceeds limit (e.g., 5 min), automatically falls back to Greedy Heuristic or Metaheuristic to guarantee a solution is delivered.
   750→
   751→2. **Data Integrity Risk:** Implement "Data Quality Gatekeepers" at the ingestion layer. Sensor data violating physical rules (e.g., temperature rising 1000°C in 1 second) should be flagged as anomalies and not used to trigger maintenance without human validation.
   752→
   753→3. **Adoption Risk:** Combat planner resistance with Explainable AI (XAI) features and HITL workflows that respect user authority.
   754→
   755→---
   756→
   757→## Human-in-the-Loop Integration
   758→
   759→Full automation is neither viable nor desirable in complex rail environments. The MMS must be designed as a decision support system that empowers human planners, not replaces them.
   760→
   761→### Visualization and Trust
   762→
   763→Algorithms cannot be black boxes. The system must visualize *why* a conflict was flagged or *why* a specific slot was chosen.
   764→
   765→**Conflict Visualization:**
   766→Instead of just displaying an "Infeasible" error, the UI should draw a red line on the Gantt chart showing exactly where the overlap or constraint violation is.
   767→
   768→**Interactive Gantt Charts:**
   769→Planners think visually. The UI should allow rescheduling via "drag-and-drop", with the system immediately recalculating and visualizing the impact of the change (delay propagation, constraint violations) in real-time.
   770→
   771→### Collaborative Planning and Veto Power
   772→
   773→**Veto Power and "Anchor & Re-plan":**
   774→In the "Automated/Person-in-Loop" scenario, the algorithm proposes a schedule, but the human planner retains veto power. If the planner "anchors" (locks) a task to a specific time, the system must re-optimize the rest of the plan around that fixed constraint.
   775→
   776→**What-If Analysis:**
   777→Planners must be able to run hypothetical scenarios (e.g., "What if we close Pit 3 for renovation next week?") and visually compare results against the base plan, seeing side-by-side impact metrics (e.g., "+2 Delayed Tasks", "+10min Overtime").
   778→
   779→### Explanation and Transparency
   780→
   781→The system should provide clear explanations for its recommendations:
   782→
   783→| Question             | System Should Explain                                   |
   784→|----------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|
   785→| "Why this train?"    | Mileage proximity to next service, availability score   |
   786→| "Why this slot?"     | Resource availability, precedence constraints satisfied |
   787→| "Why the conflict?"  | Which specific constraint is violated, by how much      |
   788→| "What's the impact?" | Downstream effects on dependent tasks                   |
   789→
   790→---
   791→
   792→## Change Propagation & Recalculation Mechanics
   793→
   794→> This section details how the system should respond when parameters change.
   795→> Critical for understanding rescheduling behavior and implementing What-If scenarios.
   796→
   797→### The Core Question
   798→
   799→**When a parameter changes, WHAT needs to be recalculated and in WHAT ORDER?**
   800→
   801→### Time Zones and Frozen Periods
   802→
   803→The system operates with hierarchical time zones that control what can be changed:
   804→
   805→| Time Horizon       | Zone Status   | Constraint Type | Allowed Changes              | Recalculation Agent       |
   806→|--------------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
   807→| T > 12 weeks       | **Liquid**    | Soft            | Structure, Volume, Frequency | Strategic Planner (MIP)   |
   808→| T-12 to T-7 weeks  | **Slushy**    | Medium          | Timing, Resource Allocation  | Tactical Planner (Hybrid) |
   809→| T-7 weeks to T-48h | **Frozen**    | Hard            | Emergency Only (High Cost)   | Change Control Committee  |
   810→| T < 48 hours       | **Execution** | Fixed           | None (break-fix only)        | Operational Controller    |
   811→
   812→**Key Insight:** The "Frozen Zone" prevents system nervousness by locking schedules close to execution.
   813→
   814→### Impact Scope by Change Type
   815→
   816→| Change Type            | Example                     | Immediate Scope              | Propagation Chain                    |
   817→|------------------------|-----------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
   818→| **Resource Capacity**  | Man-hours drop 20% in March | Local (affected tasks)       | Asset → Traffic → Service            |
   819→| **Pit Unavailability** | Pit 3 closed for 2 weeks    | Regional (depot)             | Reschedule → Backlog → Ground trains |
   820→| **Urgent Task**        | Train T045 breakdown        | Preemption of lower priority | Cascade displacement                 |
   821→| **Constraint Change**  | Annual budget cut 10%       | Global (strategic)           | Replanning required                  |
   822→| **Priority Change**    | D-Overhaul becomes critical | Single asset                 | May displace others                  |
   823→| **Cascade Delay**      | Task A delayed 2 days       | Dependency chain             | All successors shift                 |
   824→
   825→### Recalculation Strategies
   826→
   827→| Strategy                    | Trigger          | Scope                  | Latency       | Best For                    |
   828→|-----------------------------|------------------|------------------------|---------------|-----------------------------|
   829→| **Right-Shift**             | Minor delay      | Single resource        | Milliseconds  | Operational dispatch        |
   830→| **Incremental Repair**      | Resource failure | Regional (depot/route) | Seconds       | Short-term rescheduling     |
   831→| **Full Regeneration**       | Major disruption | Global (network)       | Minutes/Hours | Tactical planning / Crisis  |
   832→| **Hybrid (Shadow Account)** | Fleet limits     | Asset + Service        | Seconds/Min   | Complex rolling stock loops |
   833→
   834→### Constraint Propagation (CSP)
   835→
   836→When a constraint changes (e.g., task duration increases by 30 min):
   837→
   838→1. **Domain Reduction:** Earliest Start Time of dependent tasks increases
   839→2. **Arc Consistency:** System checks all connected dependencies
   840→3. **Chain Reaction:** Propagates until stable state (Fixed Point) or infeasibility (Empty Domain)
   841→4. **Lazy vs Eager:**
   842→   - **Eager:** Immediate propagation (always valid, but expensive)
   843→   - **Lazy:** Propagate only when queried or within time horizon (better performance)
   844→
   845→### Hierarchical Recalculation Order
   846→
   847→```
   848→Disruption occurs at Operational level (e.g., elevator broken)
   849→    │
   850→    ▼
   851→Level 1: Can we reschedule within current shift? (Incremental Repair)
   852→    │
   853→    ├── YES → Recalculate local sequence. Done.
   854→    │
   855→    └── NO → Escalate to Tactical
   856→            │
   857→            ▼
   858→        Level 2: Can we move to next week? (Slushy Zone)
   859→            │
   860→            ├── YES → Recalculate future workload.
   861→            │
   862→            └── NO → Escalate to Strategic/Emergency
   863→                    │
   864→                    ▼
   865→                Level 3: Use reserve train or cancel service?
   866→                    │
   867→                    └── Triggers Service Variation → Feeds back to strategic KPIs
   868→```
   869→
   870→### Edge Cases
   871→
   872→| Edge Case                | Description                                                         | Mitigation                                        |
   873→|--------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
   874→| **Infinite Loop**        | Constraint A pushes task to 10:00, Constraint B pushes back to 9:00 | Visited state detection + Tabu Search             |
   875→| **Infeasibility**        | No solution satisfies all hard constraints                          | Relax soft constraints, show Minimal Conflict Set |
   876→| **Simultaneous Changes** | Two users change different things at same time                      | Event Sourcing with conflict resolution           |
   877→| **Deadlock**             | Circular dependency in pit allocation                               | Cycle detection in dependency graph               |
   878→
   879→### Metrics for Recalculation Quality
   880→
   881→| Metric               | Definition                                          | Target   |
   882→|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|----------|
   883→| **Nervousness**      | How much the schedule changes after recalculation   | Minimize |
   884→| **Absorption**       | Ability to absorb small delays without rescheduling | Maximize |
   885→| **Recovery Time**    | Time to return to original plan after disruption    | Minimize |
   886→| **Passenger Impact** | Delays weighted by affected passengers              | Minimize |
   887→
   888→### Implementation: Event Sourcing
   889→
   890→Instead of updating records in place, store each change as an immutable event:
   891→
   892→```typescript
   893→// Event types for change tracking
   894→type ScheduleEvent =
   895→  | { type: 'TaskScheduled'; taskId: string; startDate: Date; pit: string }
   896→  | { type: 'TaskRescheduled'; taskId: string; oldStart: Date; newStart: Date; reason: string }
   897→  | { type: 'ConflictDetected'; taskIds: string[]; conflictType: string }
   898→  | { type: 'ConstraintOverridden'; constraintId: string; overriddenBy: string }
   899→  | { type: 'ScenarioCreated'; scenarioId: string; baselineId: string }
   900→  | { type: 'ZoneStatusChanged'; from: ZoneStatus; to: ZoneStatus };
   901→```
   902→
   903→**Benefits:**
   904→- Full audit trail
   905→- "Time travel" to any past state
   906→- Simulate multiple futures (branching)
   907→- Essential for What-If scenarios
   908→
   909→### Data Type Implications for F104
   910→
   911→From this research, the data model must include:
   912→
   913→```typescript
   914→// Zone status for tasks
   915→type ZoneStatus = 'liquid' | 'slushy' | 'frozen' | 'execution';
   916→
   917→// Constraint classification
   918→type ConstraintType = 'hard' | 'soft';
   919→
   920→// On Task entity
   921→interface Task {
   922→  // ... existing fields ...
   923→
   924→  // Change propagation fields
   925→  frozenUntil?: Date;              // When task exits frozen zone
   926→  zoneStatus: ZoneStatus;          // Current zone
   927→  constraintType: ConstraintType;  // Hard or soft
   928→
   929→  // Dependency tracking
   930→  predecessors: string[];          // Tasks that must complete before this
   931→  successors: string[];            // Tasks that depend on this
   932→
   933→  // Rescheduling metadata
   934→  originalStartDate?: Date;        // For tracking drift
   935→  rescheduledCount: number;        // How many times moved
   936→  lastRescheduledAt?: Date;
   937→  lastRescheduledReason?: string;
   938→}
   939→
   940→// Scenario for What-If
   941→interface Scenario {
   942→  id: string;
   943→  name: string;
   944→  baselineId: string;
   945→  events: ScheduleEvent[];         // Event sourcing
   946→  metrics: {
   947→    nervousness: number;           // 0-1, lower is better
   948→    costDelta: number;             // vs baseline
   949→    conflictCount: number;
   950→    delayedTasks: number;
   951→  };
   952→}
   953→```

   963→
   964→## Future Directions
   965→
   966→### Continuous Human-in-the-Loop Optimization (CHiLO)
   967→
   968→The system should learn from planner interventions. If a planner consistently overrides a specific assignment suggested by the algorithm, the system should learn this "implicit constraint" and avoid such assignments in the future.
   969→
   970→From research on adaptive scheduling:
   971→> "Systems that incorporate feedback loops from user corrections can improve recommendation accuracy by 15-25% over 6 months of operation."
   972→
   973→### Generative AI in Planning
   974→
   975→Utilize Large Language Models (LLMs) to interpret natural language constraints:
   976→- "Don't schedule Class A trains on Track 4 this week due to nearby construction"
   977→- "Prioritize Train T045 for next available pit due to passenger complaints"
   978→
   979→LLMs can convert these into mathematical constraints for the solver, reducing the need for complex configuration interfaces.
   980→
   981→### Predictive Resilience
   982→
   983→Future systems will use Digital Twins and Event Sourcing to simulate the "Ripple Effect" *before* it occurs, transforming recalculation from an emergency reaction into an anticipated strategic decision.
   984→
   985→**Key Technologies:**
   986→- Real-time mileage prediction with ML models
   987→- Sensor fusion for condition-based maintenance triggers
   988→- Automated scenario generation for stress testing
   989→
   990→### Integration Roadmap
   991→
   992→| Phase | Capability                               | Technology                      |
   993→|-------|------------------------------------------|---------------------------------|
   994→| V1    | Visual planning with manual optimization | R3F + BlueprintJS               |
   995→| V2    | Heuristic-based auto-scheduling          | OR-Tools, custom heuristics     |
   996→| V3    | Full solver integration with What-If     | MIP (Gurobi/CPLEX) + CP         |
   997→| V4    | Predictive maintenance integration       | ML models + IoT events          |
   998→| V5    | CHiLO with adaptive learning             | Feedback loops + LLM assistance |
