## 2. Railway Domain (Discovery)

> **MANDATORY READING** for all team members before starting any work.

### 2.1 Essential Glossary

| Term | Definition | System Use |
|------|------------|------------|
| **Rolling Stock** | Trains, locomotives, wagons | Main managed entity |
| **Consist** | Composition of units forming a train | A "train" can have multiple units |
| **Married Pair** | Mechanically inseparable units | Cannot be allocated separately |
| **Dead Running** | Movement without passengers/cargo | Cost to minimize |
| **Shunting** | Train maneuvering in yard/depot | Consumes 15-30 min + cost |
| **LMD** | Light Maintenance Depot | Light maintenance (PM1, PM2) |
| **HMD** | Heavy Maintenance Depot | Heavy maintenance (overhaul) |
| **PM** | Preventive Maintenance | Scheduled preventive maintenance |
| **HM** | Heavy Maintenance | Heavy maintenance |
| **Wheelset** | Wheel + axle assembly | Most critical component for balancing |
| **Bogie** | Structure with wheels under car | Highest MTTR, failure point |
| **Timetable** | Fixed schedule of services | Already exists, MMS doesn't create |
| **Service/Run** | A specific route in the timetable | MMS allocates train to service |
| **Aptitude/Homologation** | Train certification for route/country | Hard allocation constraint |

### 2.2 Business Entities

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        CONCEPTUAL MODEL                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  ┌─────────┐ belongs to  ┌─────────┐  has       ┌────────────┐  │
│  │  TRAIN  │────────────▶│  DEPOT  │◀──────────│  CAPACITY  │  │
│  └────┬────┘             └─────────┘           └────────────┘  │
│       │                                                         │
│       │ executes                                                │
│       ▼                                                         │
│  ┌─────────┐  part of    ┌───────────┐                         │
│  │ SERVICE │────────────▶│ TIMETABLE │                         │
│  └────┬────┘             └───────────┘                         │
│       │                                                         │
│       │ accumulates                                             │
│       ▼                                                         │
│  ┌─────────┐  triggers   ┌─────────────┐  occurs at            │
│  │MILEAGE  │────────────▶│ MAINTENANCE │───────────▶ DEPOT     │
│  └─────────┘             └─────────────┘                        │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### Critical Relationships

| Relationship | Cardinality | Business Rule |
|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| Train → Depot | N:1 | Train has "home" depot |
| Train → Service | N:M | One train executes various services; service can have different trains on different days |
| Train → Maintenance | 1:N | Train has maintenance history |
| Depot → Capacity | 1:1 | Each depot has limited capacity |
| Service → Timetable | N:1 | Services belong to a timetable |

### 2.3 Train Lifecycle

```
    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                    OPERATIONAL CYCLE                     │
    │                                                          │
    │   ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐             │
    │   │  DEPOT  │───▶│ SERVICE │───▶│  DEPOT  │             │
    │   │ (start) │    │  (run)  │    │  (end)  │             │
    │   └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └────┬────┘             │
    │                                      │                   │
    │        ┌─────────────────────────────┘                   │
    │        │                                                 │
    │        ▼                                                 │
    │   ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐             │
    │   │  CHECK  │───▶│  MAINT  │───▶│ RETURN  │             │
    │   │ (need?) │    │  (fix)  │    │ (ready) │             │
    │   └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘             │
    │                                                          │
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### Typical Maintenance Intervals

| Level | Interval | Duration | Lead Time | Location |
|-------|----------|----------|-----------|----------|
| PM1 | 10,000-30,000 km | 4-8 hours | 1-2 weeks | LMD |
| PM2 | 60,000-120,000 km | 1-3 days | 2-4 weeks | LMD |
| HM | 6-12 years | 2-6 weeks | 6-12 months | HMD |
| Overhaul | 12-20 years | 2-6 months | 12-24 months | HMD |

### 2.4 Unknown Unknowns (Critical Discoveries)

> Items that are not obvious but, if ignored, can invalidate the system.

#### 2.4.1 Wheelset

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Flange < 28.5mm = mandatory reprofiling | Hard constraint |
| Wear is **non-linear** | Balancing only total km is insufficient |
| Curves and climate accelerate degradation | Must weight by route profile |
| Typical interval: ~60,000 km | More frequent than PM2 |

#### 2.4.2 Bogie

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Components in series | Single point of failure |
| MTTR: 4-72 hours | Highest impact on availability |
| Wayside systems (TBOGI) detect degradation | If client has it, integrate for prediction |

#### 2.4.3 Consist (Composition)

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Trains are compositions (A+B) | Don't treat "train" as atom |
| Married pairs are inseparable | Model must track integrity |
| Coupling/decoupling has cost | Consider in optimization |

#### 2.4.4 Train Identification

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Operational ID changes at borders | Separate `physical_asset_id` from `operational_number` |
| UIC number is European standard | Use as canonical ID if applicable |

#### 2.4.5 Crew

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Train allocation is interdependent with crew | "Perfect" plan fails without driver |
| Route knowledge: driver only operates known routes | Qualification constraint |
| Traction knowledge: certification by train type | Another constraint |
| Hours of Service: legal limits | Temporal constraint |
| Ignored operational times: sign-on (15-30min), walk (5-15min), preparation (10-20min), securing (5-15min) | Adds 30-60 min/service |

**For Demo:** Model crew as simplified constraint (availability per depot/shift). Complete integration in future phase.

#### 2.4.6 Shunting

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Moving train within depot: 15-30 min | Plans that ignore this become unworkable |
| Cost: ~€350/maneuver | Must minimize |

#### 2.4.7 Homologations

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Train certified by country/network | Aptitude is matrix (train × network × service type) |
| Not a simple boolean | Data model must support |

#### 2.4.8 Infrastructure Windows

| Fact | Implication |
|------|-------------|
| Tracks have their own maintenance | Route may be blocked even with train available |
| Usually informed in advance | Integrate as route constraint |

### 2.5 What the System Does NOT Do

| Responsibility | Who Defines | MMS Only... |
|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| Maintenance intervals | Manufacturer / Regulator | Optimizes WITHIN the window |
| Timetable | Transportation Planning | Allocates train to existing service |
| Routes | Infrastructure | Uses routes as input |
| Depot capacity | Engineering | Respects as constraint |

### 2.6 Correct Mental Model

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     DECISION HIERARCHY                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  STRATEGIC (months-years)          OPERATIONAL (days-weeks)    │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐           ┌─────────────────────┐     │
│  │ When each train     │           │ Which train executes│     │
│  │ enters maintenance  │──────────▶│ which service today │     │
│  │ (macro schedule)    │ constrains│ (daily allocation)  │     │
│  └─────────────────────┘           └─────────────────────┘     │
│           │                                   │                 │
│           │ feedback if                       │                 │
│           │ infeasible                        │                 │
│           └───────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 3. Market Analysis

### 3.1 Main Competitors

#### Tier 1 — Enterprise Rail

| System | Company | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|--------|---------|-----------|------------|
| **DELMIA Quintiq** | Dassault | Market leader, end-to-end solution, advanced optimization | Very high cost, 12-24 month implementation, lock-in |
| **zedas®asset** | ZEDAS | ECM compliance specialist, strong in Germany | Less flexible, compliance focus over optimization |
| **Railfleet** | Railnova | Modern UX, telemetry and IoT focus | Doesn't focus on allocation optimization |
| **TRACS-RS** | Tracsis | Rolling stock diagrams, strong in UK | Regional, less international |

#### Tier 2 — Generic EAM

| System | Company | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|--------|---------|-----------|------------|
| **Maximo for Transportation** | IBM | Mature, enterprise-grade | Generic, requires heavy customization |
| **SAP PM / S/4HANA** | SAP | SAP ecosystem integration | Not rail-specific |

### 3.2 Market Gap

| Observation | Opportunity |
|-------------|-------------|
| No open-source solution for rail | Differentiation if considering open model |
| Km balancing not a primary feature | Can be a differentiator |
| Generally dated UX | Modern interface impresses |
| Weak Maintenance ↔ Operations integration | Unified interface is a differentiator |

### 3.3 Market Visual Patterns

Common elements in reference systems:

| Element | Use | Example |
|---------|-----|---------|
| Traffic-light status | Quick state (green/yellow/red) | Train health, depot occupancy |
| Depot cards | Cards with occupancy gauge | Main dashboard |
| Interactive Gantt | Maintenance timeline with drag-drop | Planning |
| Map with clustering | Train positions, zoom groups | Operational view |
| Alert feed | Real-time list | Sidebar or modal |
| Drill-down | Fleet → Train → Component → History | Standard navigation |

### 3.4 Proposed Differentiator for MMS

| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Km balancing as primary objective | Competitors treat as secondary |
| Unified Maintenance ↔ Operations interface | Eliminates silos, reduces rework |
| Visible dead running cost | Hidden cost made explicit |
| Accessible what-if simulation | Decision-makers can "play" |
| Visible decision justification | Trust, not black box |

## 4. Demo Strategy

### 4.1 Fundamental Principle

> **Depth > Breadth**
>
> Solving ONE pain point very well impresses more than 20 superficial features.

**Chosen pain point:** Fleet mileage balancing.

- Visually clear (comparative charts)
- Obvious financial impact (lifecycle, TCO)
- Competitive differentiator (competitors don't focus)

### 4.2 Wow Moments (What Stays in Memory)

| Moment | When | Effect on Client |
|--------|------|------------------|
| Map with "live" trains | Demo start | "They understand our operation" |
| Interactive simulation | Demo middle | Client feels ownership, interacts |
| Decision justification | Demo end | "Not a black box, I can trust" |
| Correct terminology | Throughout demo | "They know our business" |

### 4.3 Credibility Checklist

| Item | Status | Responsible |
|------|--------|-------------|
| Glossary mastered by team | ☐ | Everyone |
| Seed data with plausible names | ☐ | Data dev |
| Realistic km and intervals | ☐ | Data dev |
| Correct terminology in UI | ☐ | Designer |
| Domain FAQ prepared | ☐ | PO |
| Domain consultant consulted | ☐ | PO |

### 4.4 What to Avoid (Anti-patterns)

| Anti-pattern | Why It's Fatal | Mitigation |
|--------------|----------------|------------|
| Generic UI | "Looks like any system" | Rail-specific elements |
| Obviously fake data | "Train 1, Train 2, Depot A" | Realistic names and numbers |
| Black box | "Why did it recommend this?" | Always visible justification |
| Too many shallow features | "Doesn't really work" | Focus on 1 complete flow |
| Slow loading | "System doesn't scale" | Optimize for demo |
| Bug during presentation | "They're not ready" | Exhaustive rehearsal |

### 4.5 Scope IN (Do)

| Feature | Priority | Justification |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| Dashboard with KPIs | Must | First impression |
| Map with train positions | Must | Visual wow |
| Km balancing visualization | Must | Main pain point |
| Working allocation engine | Must | Proof of technical capability |
| Scenario simulation | Must | Client interaction |
| Recommendation justification | Must | Trust |
| Maintenance Gantt | Should | Market expectation |
| Visual alerts | Should | Demonstrates proactivity |
| PDF/Excel export | Could | Nice-to-have |

### 4.6 Scope OUT (Don't Do)

| Feature | Why Out | When to Include |
|---------|---------|-----------------|
| MILP optimization (long-term) | Complexity, 3 months insufficient | Phase 2 |
| Real EAM integration | Depends on client environment | Phase 2 |
| Disruption management | Different architecture, huge scope | Phase 3 |
| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
| Native mobile | Responsive web sufficient | If client demands |
| Multi-language | English sufficient for demo | Production |

## 5. Functional Requirements

### 5.1 Modules and Features

#### 5.1.1 Dashboard (Must Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Fleet KPIs | Total trains, available, in maintenance, % utilization | Numbers updated in < 5s |
| Depot occupancy | Gauge per depot showing current vs capacity | Speedometer-type visual |
| Active alerts | List of critical alerts (over/under-running, upcoming maintenance) | Sorted by criticality |
| Km variance | Fleet km distribution indicator | Green (< 10%), yellow (10-20%), red (> 20%) |

#### 5.1.2 Map (Must Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Train positions | Icons on map showing location | Can be last known position |
| Depots | Depot markers with occupancy | Click opens details |
| Routes | Lines connecting points | Simplified for demo |
| Clustering | Grouping on zoom out | Number indicates quantity |

**Note:** For demo, positions can be simulated/mocked. No real GPS integration required.

#### 5.1.3 Km Balancing (Must Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Distribution chart | Histogram or box plot of fleet | Shows mean, outliers |
| Train ranking | List sorted by km (over/under-running) | Top 10 each extreme |
| Trend | Temporal chart showing evolution | Last 6 months |
| Drill-down | Click on train opens details | Km and service history |

#### 5.1.4 Allocation (Must Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Requirements input | Select service and date | Simple form |
| Ranked recommendation | List of trains sorted by suitability | Top 5 with scores |
| Justification | Why each train was ranked that way | Readable text |
| Apply allocation | Confirm choice | Updates state |

**Justification Example:**
```
Recommendation: Train T-1234 for Service S-789

Reasons:
• Km 12% below fleet average (balancing priority)
• Service ends 5km from maintenance depot
• PM scheduled in 3 days, destination depot has available slot
• Aptitude confirmed for Lisbon-Porto route

Score: 94/100
```

#### 5.1.5 Simulation / What-If (Must Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Create scenario | Duplicate current state for sandbox | Isolated scenario |
| Modify parameters | Change allocations, maintenance dates | Drag-drop or form interface |
| Compare results | Side-by-side KPIs | Comparison table |
| Projection | Show impact in 30/60/90 days | Trend charts |

#### 5.1.6 Maintenance Gantt (Should Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Visual timeline | Bars per train showing maintenances | Horizontal scroll |
| Drag-drop | Move maintenance to another date | Updates and validates conflicts |
| Colors by type | PM1, PM2, HM differentiated | Visible legend |
| Conflicts | Visual highlight if depot full | Red border |

#### 5.1.7 Alerts (Should Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Real-time feed | Updating alert list | WebSocket or 30s polling |
| Categories | Over-running, under-running, maintenance, capacity | Filter by type |
| Quick action | Link to resolve (go to train, allocation) | 1 click |

#### 5.1.8 Export (Could Have)

| Feature | Description | Acceptance Criteria |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| PDF | Fleet status report | Professional layout |
| Excel | Km and maintenance data | Organized columns |

### 5.2 Main User Stories

```
US-01: As a Planner, I want to visualize fleet km distribution
       to identify over/under-running trains.

US-02: As a Planner, I want to receive a recommendation for which train to allocate
       to a service, with visible justification.

US-03: As a Planner, I want to simulate allocation scenarios to compare
       impact before deciding.

US-04: As a Dispatcher, I want to see current train positions on the map
       for quick decisions.

US-05: As a Manager, I want a dashboard with KPIs for executive view
       without needing to navigate multiple screens.

US-06: As a Planner, I want to see maintenance timeline in Gantt
       to identify capacity conflicts.
```

## 6. Non-Functional Requirements

### 6.1 Performance

| Requirement | Target | Justification |
|-------------|--------|---------------|
| Allocation time | < 30 seconds for 100 trains | Dispatcher won't wait |
| Screen loading | < 3 seconds | Credibility |
| Map update | < 5 seconds | "Real-time" feeling |
| Scenario simulation | < 10 seconds | Interactivity |

### 6.2 Scalability (for Demo)

| Dimension | Demo Target | Production (future) |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Trains | 80-120 | 500+ |
| Depots | 12-15 | 50+ |
| Services/day | 200-300 | 2000+ |
| Concurrent users | 5-10 | 100+ |

### 6.3 Availability

For demo: **N/A** — not a production system.

Actual requirement: **Work for 15 minutes without crashing during presentation.**

### 6.4 Security

For demo: **Minimal** — basic authentication sufficient.

### 6.5 Data

| Requirement | Specification |
|-------------|---------------|
| Seed data | 80-120 trains, 12-15 depots, 6 months of history |
| Realism | Plausible names, coherent km, correct intervals |
| Reset | Ability to reset to demo initial state |

## 7. UX/UI Specification

### 7.1 Personas

#### Persona 1: Maintenance Planner

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Name | Paul, 45 years old |
| Goal | Schedule maintenances minimizing operational impact |
| Frequency | Daily, 4-8 hours in system |
| Device | Desktop, large monitor |
| Frustrations | Excel, lack of visibility, rework |
| Critical requirement | Gantt with drag-drop |

#### Persona 2: Dispatcher (Operations Controller)

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Name | Diana, 38 years old |
| Goal | Allocate trains to services in real-time |
| Frequency | Continuous during shift |
| Device | Desktop, multiple monitors |
| Frustrations | Slow system, lack of information |
| Critical requirement | Response < 3 seconds |

#### Persona 3: Fleet Manager

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Name | Gabriel, 52 years old |
| Goal | Monitor KPIs, make strategic decisions |
| Frequency | Weekly, 30-60 minutes |
| Device | Desktop or tablet |
| Frustrations | Scattered data, lack of drill-down |
| Critical requirement | Dashboard with executive view |

### 7.2 Critical Journeys

#### Journey 1: Allocate Train to Service

```
1. Dispatcher receives demand (service X needs train)
2. Opens Allocation screen
3. Selects service and date
4. System shows ranked recommendations with justification
5. Dispatcher reviews top 3 options
6. Selects preferred train
7. Confirms allocation
8. System updates state and notifies

Expected time: < 2 minutes
Clicks: < 5
```

#### Journey 2: Identify and Resolve Imbalance

```
1. Planner opens Dashboard
2. Sees km variance indicator in yellow/red
3. Clicks to see balancing details
4. Identifies most under-running trains
5. Opens scenario simulation
6. Adjusts future allocations to prioritize under-running
7. Compares projected impact
8. Applies changes

Expected time: < 10 minutes
```

#### Journey 3: Plan Maintenance

```
1. Planner opens Maintenance Gantt
2. Sees next 4 weeks
3. Identifies conflict (depot full in week 2)
4. Drag-drop a maintenance to week 3
5. System validates (no conflict)
6. Saves change

Expected time: < 5 minutes
```

#### 7.3.2 Reusable Components

| Component | Use | Main Props |
|-----------|-----|------------|
| `<StatusBadge>` | Green/yellow/red indicator | status, label |
| `<DepotCard>` | Depot card with gauge | name, capacity, current |
| `<TrainRow>` | Train row in list | train, km, status, actions |
| `<KPICard>` | Metric card | title, value, trend, icon |
| `<GanttBar>` | Bar in timeline | start, end, type, draggable |
| `<MapMarker>` | Marker on map | position, type, tooltip |
| `<JustificationCard>` | Recommendation explanation | reasons[], score |

#### 7.3.3 Colors and Semantics

| Color | Use | Hex |
|-------|-----|-----|
| Green | OK, within expected | #22C55E |
| Yellow | Attention, near limit | #EAB308 |
| Red | Critical, action needed | #EF4444 |
| Blue | Information, links | #3B82F6 |
| Gray | Neutral, disabled | #6B7280 |

### 7.4 Anti-patterns to Avoid

| Anti-pattern | Problem | Solution |
|--------------|---------|----------|
| Read-only dashboard | Planner can't act | Inline actions |
| Loading spinner > 3s | Seems slow | Skeleton + progressive loading |
| Data without context | "150,000 km" — is it good or bad? | Compare with average, show status |
| Modal over modal | Confusing UX | Maximum 1 modal level |
| Giant tables | Infinite scroll | Pagination + filters + search |
| Decorative charts | Not informative | Every chart should have associated action |

### 8.2 Technology Stack

| Layer | Technology | Justification |
|-------|------------|---------------|
| Frontend | Next.js 14 + React 18 + TypeScript | SSR, productivity, typing |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui | Ready components, consistency |
| State | Zustand or React Query | Simplicity |
| API | Node.js + Fastify + TypeScript | Performance, native typing |
| Solver | Python 3.11 + OR-Tools | Mature optimization library |
| Database | PostgreSQL 16 | Reliable, JSONB for flexibility |
| Time-series | TimescaleDB | If telemetry history needed |
| Cache | Redis | Session state, queues, pub/sub for alerts |
| Monorepo | Turborepo | Incremental builds |
| Maps | Mapbox or Leaflet | Mapbox prettier, Leaflet free |
| Charts | Recharts or Chart.js | React integration |
| Gantt | react-gantt-timeline or custom | Evaluate existing libs |

### 8.3 Data Model

#### 8.3.1 Main Entities

```sql
-- Train (physical asset)
CREATE TABLE trains (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    physical_asset_id VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,  -- Permanent ID
    operational_number VARCHAR(50),                  -- Can change
    train_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    home_depot_id UUID REFERENCES depots(id),
    current_depot_id UUID REFERENCES depots(id),
    total_km DECIMAL(12,2) DEFAULT 0,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'available',          -- available, in_service, maintenance, out_of_service
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Depot
CREATE TABLE depots (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    code VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    type VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,                       -- LMD, HMD
    capacity INTEGER NOT NULL,
    latitude DECIMAL(10,7),
    longitude DECIMAL(10,7),
    valid_from DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    valid_to DATE DEFAULT '9999-12-31',              -- Temporal versioning
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Service (from timetable)
CREATE TABLE services (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    service_number VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    origin_depot_id UUID REFERENCES depots(id),
    destination_depot_id UUID REFERENCES depots(id),
    distance_km DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    departure_time TIME NOT NULL,
    arrival_time TIME NOT NULL,
    days_of_week INTEGER[] DEFAULT '{1,2,3,4,5,6,7}',
    train_type_required VARCHAR(50),
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Allocation (train → service)
CREATE TABLE allocations (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_id UUID REFERENCES trains(id) NOT NULL,
    service_id UUID REFERENCES services(id) NOT NULL,
    date DATE NOT NULL,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'planned',            -- planned, confirmed, completed, cancelled
    allocated_by VARCHAR(50),                        -- user or 'system'
    allocation_reason JSONB,                         -- Justification
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    UNIQUE(service_id, date)
);

-- Maintenance
CREATE TABLE maintenances (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_id UUID REFERENCES trains(id) NOT NULL,
    depot_id UUID REFERENCES depots(id) NOT NULL,
    type VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,                       -- PM1, PM2, HM, OVERHAUL
    scheduled_date DATE NOT NULL,
    scheduled_duration_hours INTEGER NOT NULL,
    actual_start TIMESTAMPTZ,
    actual_end TIMESTAMPTZ,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'scheduled',          -- scheduled, in_progress, completed, cancelled
    km_at_maintenance DECIMAL(12,2),
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Mileage history (for temporal analysis)
CREATE TABLE mileage_history (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_id UUID REFERENCES trains(id) NOT NULL,
    date DATE NOT NULL,
    km_accumulated DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL,
    km_delta DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    UNIQUE(train_id, date)
);

-- Aptitudes (homologations)
CREATE TABLE train_aptitudes (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_id UUID REFERENCES trains(id) NOT NULL,
    network VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,                    -- 'Portugal', 'Spain', etc.
    service_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,               -- 'passenger', 'freight', 'high_speed'
    valid_from DATE NOT NULL,
    valid_to DATE DEFAULT '9999-12-31',
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Maintenance plans (versioned)
CREATE TABLE maintenance_plans (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    maintenance_type VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    interval_km DECIMAL(10,2),
    interval_days INTEGER,
    tolerance_percent DECIMAL(5,2) DEFAULT 10,
    duration_hours INTEGER NOT NULL,
    version INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    valid_from DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    valid_to DATE DEFAULT '9999-12-31',
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

#### 8.3.2 Critical Modeling Decisions

| Decision | Choice | Justification |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Train ID | UUID + separate physical_asset_id | Operational number changes |
| Km | DECIMAL(12,2) | Avoids rounding error |
| Timestamps | TIMESTAMPTZ (always UTC) | Cross-timezone operations |
| Depot | Temporal versioning | Depots change over time |
| Aptitude | Separate table (matrix) | Not a simple boolean |
| Allocation justification | JSONB | Flexible structure |

### 8.4 Solver (Optimization Engine)

#### 8.4.1 Technology

- **Library:** Google OR-Tools (CP-SAT solver)
- **Language:** Python
- **Communication:** gRPC with Node.js API

#### 8.4.2 Optimization Model (Simplified for Demo)

**Decision variables:**
- `x[t,s]` ∈ {0,1}: train `t` is allocated to service `s`

**Objective function (minimize):**
```
minimize:
    α * km_variance                    -- Balancing
  + β * Σ dead_running_km              -- Empty movements
  + γ * Σ (km_above_average)²          -- Penalizes over-running
```

**Constraints:**
```
1. Each service has exactly 1 train
   Σ_t x[t,s] = 1  ∀s

2. Train can only do 1 service at a time (no temporal overlap)

3. Train must be available (not in maintenance)

4. Train must have aptitude for the route

5. Previous service destination depot = next origin depot
   (or account for dead running)

6. Depot capacity respected
```

#### 8.4.3 Solver API

```python
# solver/service.py

from ortools.sat.python import cp_model
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AllocationRequest:
    services: list[Service]
    trains: list[Train]
    date: str
    constraints: dict

@dataclass
class AllocationResult:
    allocations: list[Allocation]
    score: float
    solve_time_ms: int
    justifications: dict[str, list[str]]

def solve_allocation(request: AllocationRequest) -> AllocationResult:
    model = cp_model.CpModel()

    # Create variables
    x = {}
    for t in request.trains:
        for s in request.services:
            x[t.id, s.id] = model.NewBoolVar(f'x_{t.id}_{s.id}')

    # Add constraints
    # ... (detailed implementation)

    # Solve
    solver = cp_model.CpSolver()
    solver.parameters.max_time_in_seconds = 30
    status = solver.Solve(model)

    # Extract results and justifications
    # ...

    return AllocationResult(...)
```

#### 8.4.4 Justification Generation

```python
def generate_justification(train: Train, service: Service, context: dict) -> list[str]:
    reasons = []

    fleet_avg_km = context['fleet_avg_km']
    km_diff_pct = (train.total_km - fleet_avg_km) / fleet_avg_km * 100

    if km_diff_pct < -10:
        reasons.append(f"Km {abs(km_diff_pct):.0f}% below average (balancing priority)")
    elif km_diff_pct > 10:
        reasons.append(f"⚠️ Km {km_diff_pct:.0f}% above average")

    if service.destination_depot_id == train.next_maintenance_depot_id:
        reasons.append("Service ends near maintenance depot")

    if train.days_to_maintenance <= 7:
        reasons.append(f"Maintenance scheduled in {train.days_to_maintenance} days")

    return reasons
```

### 8.6 Cache Strategy

| Data | TTL | Invalidation |

| Train positions | 30 sec | Polling |

### 8.7 API ↔ Solver Communication

**Chosen option:** gRPC

**Justification:**
- Strong typing (protobuf)
- Superior performance to REST for complex data
- Streaming support (useful for solver progress)

**Fallback:** REST with 45-second timeout.

```protobuf
// solver.proto
service AllocationSolver {
    rpc Recommend(AllocationRequest) returns (AllocationResponse);
    rpc Simulate(SimulationRequest) returns (SimulationResponse);
}

message AllocationRequest {
    repeated Service services = 1;
    repeated Train trains = 2;
    string date = 3;
    Constraints constraints = 4;
}
```

### 11.2 Contingency Plans

#### R1: Seem Generic

**Triggers:** Negative validation feedback, incorrect terms in UI.

4. Redo seed with consultant data

#### R4: Bug During Demo

**Triggers:** Crash in rehearsal.

2. Create fallback screens (PNG/PDF)

2. Assesses timeline impact

## 12. Presentation Script

### 12.1 Structure (15 minutes)

| Min | Section | Action | Objective |
|-----|---------|--------|-----------|

| 1-3 | Context | "We understand your problem" | Credibility |

| 5-7 | Map | Trains, depots, clustering | Visual wow |

| 9-11 | Allocation | Recommendation + justification | Engine working |
| 11-13 | Simulation | Interactive what-if | Client interacts |
| 13-14 | Transparency | "Not a black box" | Trust |
| 14-15 | Closing | Next steps | Call to action |

### 12.2 Script by Section

#### Opening (0-1 min)

> "Good morning. Today we'll show how MMS solves the challenge of balancing fleet utilization while maintaining operational availability."

#### Context (1-3 min)

> "We spoke with sector operators and identified three main pain points:
> 1. Over-running trains degrade faster, increasing maintenance cost
> 2. Under-running trains represent idle capital
> 3. Manual planning in Excel doesn't scale
>
> MMS addresses all three points."

#### Dashboard (3-5 min)

**Action:** Show dashboard screen.

> "This is the executive view. In seconds you see:
> - 98 of 112 trains available
> - Km variance at 18% — above the ideal 10%
> - 2 depots near capacity
>
> Red here indicates action needed."

#### Map (5-7 min)

**Action:** Navigate to map, zoom in/out.

> "Here we see each train's current position. Zoom out groups automatically. Clicking on a depot, we see stationed trains and occupancy."

#### Balancing (7-9 min)

**Action:** Show distribution chart.

> "This chart shows km distribution. See these 5 trains well above average — they're priority candidates for stopping. And these 4 below — we should allocate more."

#### Allocation (9-11 min)

**Action:** Execute live allocation.

> "Let's allocate a train for tomorrow's IC 501 service."
> [Selects service, clicks Recommend]
> "The system suggests train UTE-3400-012. Why? See the justification:
> - 12% below average — needs to run more
> - Ends in Porto, which has maintenance slot in 3 days
> - Aptitude confirmed for the line"

#### Simulation (11-13 min)

**Action:** Create scenario, adjust, compare.

> "What if we want to test another strategy? We create a parallel scenario..."
> [Shows side-by-side]
> "We see the alternative reduces variance by 3% but increases dead running by 8%. The decision is yours, with data."

#### Transparency (13-14 min)

> "Important: MMS is not a black box. Every recommendation shows the why. You maintain control, the system helps you decide."

#### Closing (14-15 min)

> "In summary: visible balancing, intelligent allocation with justification, simulation for informed decision. Questions?"

## 13. Appendices

### Appendix A: Complete Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| Allocation | Assignment of train to service |
| Aptitude | Train qualification for route/service type |
| Bogie | Structure with wheels under the car |
| Consist | Composition of units forming train |
| Dead running | Empty train movement (no passengers/cargo) |
| Depot | Parking and/or maintenance location |
| ECM | Entity in Charge of Maintenance — maintenance responsible |
| EAM | Enterprise Asset Management — asset management system |
| HM | Heavy Maintenance |
| HMD | Heavy Maintenance Depot |
| LMD | Light Maintenance Depot |
| Married pair | Mechanically inseparable train units |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Repair |
| Overhaul | General revision (most complete) |
| PM | Preventive Maintenance |
| Rolling stock | Trains, locomotives, wagons |
| Service | A specific route in the timetable |
| Shunting | Train maneuvering in yard |
| Timetable | Service schedule |
| Wheelset | Wheel + axle assembly |

### Appendix B: Domain FAQ (For Team)

| Question | Answer |
|----------|--------|
| Why not call it "car" or "vehicle"? | Technical term is "rolling stock". |
| What's the difference between LMD and HMD? | LMD does light maintenance (PM1, PM2). HMD does heavy (overhaul). |
| What is "dead running"? | Train running empty. Has fuel/energy cost and wear without revenue. |
| Why is km balancing important? | Uniform wear = equal lifecycle = synchronized new fleet purchase = savings. |
| What is "consist"? | The composition of units forming a train. Can change. |
| What are "married pairs"? | Units that cannot be separated (permanent mechanical connection). |

## Documentos Analisados:

1. **README.md** - Visão geral do domínio MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)
2. **MMS_PROFESSIONAL_PROFILES.md** - Perfis profissionais necessários para o projeto
3. **MMS_DEMO_PLAYBOOK.md** - Playbook completo para criar a demo
4. **BMAD_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md** - Plano de implementação usando metodologia BMAD
5. **OLD_LEGACY_RESEARCH.md** - Pesquisa extensa sobre sistemas de manutenção ferroviária
6. **RFQ.pdf** - O documento oficial da RFQ (Request for Quotation) do cliente

## Resumo do Projeto:

**O que é:** Sistema de Gerenciamento de Quilometragem para Metro (MMS - Mileage Management System) para o Dubai Metro, operado pela Keolis-MHI.

**Cliente:** RTA (Roads and Transport Authority) de Dubai

**Contexto:**
- 128 trens total (79 Kinki Sharyo + 50 Alstom - nota: a RFQ diz 49+79=128, mas a pesquisa corrigiu para 79+50=129)
- Atualmente usam IBM Maximo para gestão de ativos, mas não para quilometragem
- Gestão de quilometragem é feita manualmente via Excel
- Precisam de solução automatizada, baseada em dados

**Objetivo:**


Desenvolveremos uma demo estratégica em três meses para conquistar a licitação, focando em impressionar os decisores-chave com um sistema que otimize o balanceamento da frota de trens. O projeto terá três módulos centrais para demonstrar valor: planejamento de manutenção, alocação operacional e gestão de recursos, mostrando como podemos transformar processos manuais em uma solução tecnológica inteligente.

A abordagem técnica combina tecnologias modernas: Next.js para frontend, Node.js no backend, Python com OR-Tools para resolução de problemas complexos, e PostgreSQL como banco de dados. Utilizaremos uma metodologia ágil chamada BMAD, que prioriza construção rápida e arquitetura inovadora, dividindo o projeto em fases progressivas de gestão de quilometragem, modelagem financeira e expansão para múltiplas frotas.

O desenvolvimento será estruturado em 6 sprints ao longo de 12 semanas, permitindo iterações rápidas e ajustes constantes no projeto.

content.text:
# Análise Completa do Projeto MMS

## Resumo Executivo

### O que é o Projeto?

**MMS - Metro Mileage Management System** é um sistema de gestão de quilometragem para o **Metro de Dubai**, operado pela **Keolis-MHI** (joint venture entre Keolis e Mitsubishi Heavy Industries).

### O Problema a Resolver

| Situação Atual | Impacto |
|----------------|---------|
| Sem ferramenta dedicada para gestão de quilometragem | Risco de não-conformidade |
| IBM Maximo apenas para gestão de ativos | Não faz otimização de quilometragem |
| Gestão manual via **Excel** | Ineficiência, erros, atrasos |
| Dados de quilometragem em pastas manuais | Sem integração, sem automação |
| Pouca integração Manutenção ↔ Operações | Decisões isoladas |
| Sem simulações preditivas | Incapacidade de avaliar cenários |

### A Frota

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          DUBAI METRO FLEET              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Kinki Sharyo: 79 trens                 │
│  Alstom:       50 trens                 │
│  ─────────────────────────              │
│  TOTAL:        129 trens                │
│                                         │
│  Contrato: 12 anos de manutenção        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## O Que Estamos Fazendo

### Objetivo Estratégico

**Criar uma DEMO funcional em 3 meses** para vencer a licitação de software do RTA Dubai.

### Critério de Sucesso

> "Após 15 minutos de demo, o cliente pensa: **'Eles entendem nosso problema melhor que os outros.'**"

### Três Módulos Principais (da RFQ)

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ESCOPO DO SISTEMA MMS                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  1. MAINTENANCE PLANNING                                        │
│     • Buscar quilometragem diária de cada trem                 │
│     • Prever cronogramas de manutenção baseados em km          │
│     • Ajustar schedule com recursos disponíveis                │
│     • Planos curto prazo (semanal/mensal) e longo (1-10 anos)  │
│     • Alertas de underrun/overrun de quilometragem             │
│     • Simulações de cenários                                   │
│                                                                 │
│  2. OPERATIONAL PLANNING                                        │
│     • Recomendar trens para uso diário (fitness + km)          │
│     • Recomendar run assignments (rede + quilometragem)        │
│     • Alertas de desvio previsto vs. real                      │
│     • Interface Manutenção ↔ Operações                         │
│                                                                 │
│  3. RESOURCE PLANNING                                           │
│     • Considerar manpower, spare costs, tracks (fixos)         │
│     • Evitar over/underutilização nos 12 anos                  │
│     • Custeio por frota baseado em km e plano                  │
│     • Diferentes estratégias de gestão de km                   │
│                                                                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Fases de Entrega (RFQ)

| Fase | Escopo | Status Demo |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **Phase 1** | Mileage management + Run assignments | **FOCO DA DEMO** |
| Phase 2 | Costing/Financial modeling | Futuro |
| Phase 3 | Frotas adicionais + Competence Mgmt | Futuro |

---

## Stack Técnica Proposta

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ARQUITETURA MMS DEMO                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────┐                                                │
│   │   BROWSER   │  Next.js 14 + React 18 + TypeScript           │
│   │  + shadcn/ui│  + Tailwind CSS + Mapbox/Leaflet              │
│   └──────┬──────┘                                                │
│          │ HTTP/WS                                               │
│          ▼                                                       │
│   ┌─────────────┐         ┌─────────────┐                       │
│   │   API       │         │   SOLVER    │                       │
│   │  (Fastify)  │◄───────▶│  (Python)   │  OR-Tools (CP-SAT)    │
│   │  Node.js    │  gRPC   │             │                       │
│   └──────┬──────┘         └─────────────┘                       │
│          │                                                       │
│          ▼                                                       │
│   ┌─────────────┐                                                │
│   │   Redis     │  Cache + Pub/Sub                              │
│   └──────┬──────┘                                                │
│          │                                                       │
│          ▼                                                       │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                       │
│   │    PostgreSQL 16 + TimescaleDB      │                       │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────┘                       │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Diferenciadores da Demo

| Diferenciador | Por que importa |
|---------------|-----------------|
| **Balanceamento de km como objetivo primário** | Concorrentes tratam como secundário |
| **Interface unificada Manutenção ↔ Operações** | Elimina silos, reduz retrabalho |
| **Custo de dead running visível** | Custo oculto tornado explícito |
| **Simulação what-if acessível** | Decisores podem "brincar" |
| **Justificativa visível das decisões** | Confiança, não caixa preta |

---

# MMS Project Context

> BMAD Project Configuration for Metro Mileage Management System

---

## Project Identity

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Name** | MMS - Metro Mileage Management System |
| **Type** | Demo for enterprise railway fleet management bid |
| **Timeline** | 12 weeks (3 months) |
| **Track** | BMad Method (Level 2-3) |
| **Goal** | Win railway fleet management bid by impressing client in 15-minute demo |

---

## Configuration

### Language Settings

| Setting | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Communication Language** | Portuguese (pt-BR) |
| **Code Language** | English |
| **Document Output Language** | English |
| **User Skill Level** | Expert |

### Path Configuration

| Path | Location |
|------|----------|
| **Project Root** | `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms/MFC` |
| **Planning Artifacts** | `./bmad-artifacts/` |
| **Implementation Artifacts** | `./bmad-artifacts/phase-4-implementation/` |
| **Knowledge Base** | `./bmad-knowledge/` |
| **Source Code** | `./src/` |
| **Existing Documentation** | `./docs/` |

---

## Key Reference Documents

| Document | Path | Purpose |
|----------|------|---------|
| **Domain Reference** | `./docs/MMS_DEMO_PLAYBOOK.md` | Complete playbook with domain, requirements, architecture |
| **Professional Profiles** | `./docs/MMS_PROFESSIONAL_PROFILES.md` | Team roles and hiring requirements |
| **Domain Overview** | `./docs/README.md` | High-level system description |
| **Implementation Plan** | `./docs/BMAD_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md` | BMAD integration plan |
| **RFQ** | `./docs/RFQ.pdf` | Original request for quotation |

---

## Domain Context

### System Purpose

MMS (Metro Mileage Management System) is an enterprise solution to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets (>100 units), bridging Operations and Maintenance via predictive, data-driven planning integrated with EAM.

### Key Domain Concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Rolling Stock** | Railway vehicles (trains, carriages) requiring maintenance |
| **Fleet** | Group of rolling stock sharing same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile |
| **Mileage Balancing** | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet, preventing underrun/overrun |
| **Run Assignment** | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes based on fitness and mileage targets |
| **PM (Preventive Maintenance)** | Scheduled maintenance at defined km intervals (PM1, PM2) |
| **HM (Heavy Maintenance)** | Major overhauls at extended intervals |

### Primary Pain Point

**Mileage imbalance across fleet** leads to:
- Over-utilized trains degrading faster (increased maintenance costs)
- Under-utilized trains representing idle capital
- Manual Excel-based planning that doesn't scale

---

## Success Criteria

### Demo Success Metrics

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Demo Duration | 15 minutes without crash |
| Client Impression | "They understand our problem" |
| Domain Accuracy | 100% correct railway terminology |
| Features Working | All Must-Have features functional |

### Wow Moments (from Playbook §4.2)

1. **Map with live trains** - Visual impact at demo start
2. **Interactive simulation** - Client engagement in middle
3. **Decision justification** - Trust building at end

└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Plano de Ação Detalhado

### SEMANA 1-2: Setup + Analysis (Fase 0-1)

#### Dia 1-2: Completar Setup Técnico

| # | Tarefa | Prioridade | Output |
|---|--------|------------|--------|
| 1 | **Inicializar monorepo** (Turborepo) | CRÍTICA | `package.json`, `turbo.json` |
| 2 | **Criar packages**: frontend, backend, solver | CRÍTICA | Estrutura de pastas |
| 3 | **Setup Next.js 14** com App Router | CRÍTICA | `/src/frontend/` |
| 4 | **Setup Fastify** com TypeScript | CRÍTICA | `/src/backend/` |
| 5 | **Setup Python** environment para solver | CRÍTICA | `/src/solver/` |
| 6 | **Setup PostgreSQL** via Docker | CRÍTICA | `docker-compose.yml` |
| 7 | **Configurar ESLint/Prettier** | ALTA | Config files |

```bash

│   ├── frontend/            # Next.js 14

│   │   ├── next.config.js
│   │   └── app/

└── ...
```

#### Dia 3-5: Formalizar Análise (BMAD Phase 1)

**MILESTONE:** Demo pronta para apresentação.

---

---

## Riscos e Mitigações

| Risco | Probabilidade | Impacto | Mitigação |
|-------|---------------|---------|-----------|
| **Parecer genérico** | Alta | Fatal | Terminologia + seed realista + consultor |
| **Solver lento** | Média | Alto | Timeout + cache + limitar dados |
| **Bug na demo** | Média | Alto | Fallback + rehearsal exaustivo |
| **Scope creep** | Alta | Médio | PO como guardião, escopo fechado |

content.text:
## Resumo Final

### Sobre o Projeto MMS

O **MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** é um sistema para gerenciar quilometragem de frotas de metrô para o **Dubai Metro**, operado pela Keolis-MHI. O projeto visa:

1. **Substituir gestão manual via Excel** por sistema automatizado
2. **Balancear quilometragem** da frota (129 trens: 79 Kinki Sharyo + 50 Alstom)
3. **Integrar Manutenção ↔ Operações** em uma única interface
4. **Prever e otimizar** manutenção preventiva

### O que Estamos Fazendo

**Criando uma DEMO funcional em 12 semanas** para vencer uma licitação. A documentação está excelente e completa; falta executar o código.

### Estado Atual

- **Documentação:** ✅ 100% completa (Playbook, Profiles, Research, BMAD Plan)
- **Código:** ❌ 0% (estrutura de pastas criada, mas vazia)
- **Fase BMAD:** Phase 0 (Setup) - parcialmente completo

### Próximo Passo Imediato

**Inicializar o monorepo** com Turborepo e criar a estrutura de código (Next.js + Fastify + Python/OR-Tools).

---

Quer que eu comece a implementar o setup técnico (monorepo, Docker, estrutura de packages)?

content.content:
# BMAD Method: Complete Overview

## What is BMAD?

**BMAD** (Build More, Architect Dreams) is an AI-driven agile development framework featuring 21 specialized agents across 4 modules and 50+ guided workflows. It scales from simple bug fixes to enterprise platforms.

## Installation

```bash
# V6 Alpha (recommended)
npx bmad-method@alpha install

# V4 Stable (production)
npx bmad-method install
```

After installation, run `*workflow-init` in your IDE to analyze your project and receive recommendations.

## Core Architecture

### BMad Core Foundation
The system relies on **"CORE"** (Collaboration Optimized Reflection Engine), providing the universal framework for human-AI collaboration supporting custom domain solutions.

### Three Official Modules

1. **BMad Method (BMM)** - 12 agents, 34 workflows across 4 development phases
2. **BMad Builder (BMB)** - Create custom agents, workflows, and domain-specific modules
3. **Creative Intelligence Suite (CIS)** - Innovation and problem-solving workflows

## The 4-Phase Methodology

1. **Analysis** - Brainstorming, research, solution exploration
2. **Planning** - PRDs, tech specs, design documents
3. **Solutioning** - Architecture, UX, technical approach
4. **Implementation** - Story-driven development with validation

## Specialized Agents (12 Total)

**Development:** Developer, UX Designer, Tech Writer
**Architecture:** Architect, Test Architect, Game Architect
**Product:** PM, Analyst, Game Designer
**Leadership:** Scrum Master, BMad Master, Game Developer

## Three Workflow Tracks

| Track | Purpose | Planning | Duration |
|-------|---------|----------|----------|
| ⚡ Quick Flow | Bug fixes, small features | Tech spec | <5 min |
| 📋 BMad Method | Products, platforms | Full suite | <15 min |
| 🏢 Enterprise | Compliance, scale | Governance | <30 min |

## Key Features

- **Scale-adaptive intelligence** adjusts complexity automatically
- **Customizable agents** with modifiable personalities and expertise
- **Update-safe customization** preserving user configurations
- **Multi-language support** separating communication from code output
- **Document sharding** reducing token usage by 90%
- **Web bundles** for ChatGPT, Claude Projects, Gemini Gems
- **Custom module installation** with dependency management

## Repository Structure

```
.github/          - Configuration files
docs/             - Documentation hub
src/              - Source code for modules
test/             - Test files
samples/          - Example custom modules
tools/            - Development utilities
website/          - Web resources
```

## V6 Major Improvements from V4

- Complete architectural overhaul
- Visual workflow diagrams
- 50+ workflows (up from 20)
- BMad Builder for custom solutions
- Enhanced agent customization
- Community marketplace preparation

## Support & Community

- **Discord:** Active community collaboration
- **GitHub Issues:** Bug reports and feature requests
- **YouTube:** Video tutorials and demonstrations
- **Web Bundles:** Pre-built agent packages

## Requirements

- Node.js 20.0.0 or higher
- MIT License (open source)

## Development Commands

```bash
npm test          # Run quality checks
npm run lint:fix  # Fix code style
npm run format:fix # Auto-format
npm run bundle    # Build web bundles
```

The system emphasizes structured, methodology-driven AI collaboration rather than generic code assistance, with specialized expertise across product management, architecture, testing, and leadership roles.

# BMAD-METHOD Documentation Files

Based on the repository structure, the docs folder contains the following documentation:

## Available Guides

1. **Getting Started** - A directory with introductory materials
2. **Bundle Distribution Setup** - Instructions for distributing bundles
3. **Agent Customization Guide** - Guidance on customizing agents
4. **Custom Content Installation** - Steps for installing custom content
5. **Custom Content** - General custom content documentation
6. **Document Sharding Guide** - Information on sharding documents
7. **Index** - Main documentation index

content.content:
# BMad Method & BMad Core - Complete Overview

## Project Summary

**BMad Method** is an AI-driven agile development framework built on **BMad Core** (Collaboration Optimized Reflection Engine). It features 21 specialized AI agents across multiple modules, 50+ guided workflows, and scale-adaptive intelligence ranging from bug fixes to enterprise platforms.

## Installation

```bash
# Install v6 Alpha (recommended)
npx bmad-method@alpha install

# Or stable v4 for production
npx bmad-method install
```

## Getting Started (3 Steps)

1. **Install** - Use NPX command above
2. **Initialize** - Load any agent in your IDE and run `*workflow-init`
3. **Choose Track** - System analyzes your project and recommends:
   - ⚡ **Quick Flow** - Bug fixes, small features (< 5 min)
   - 📋 **BMad Method** - Products, platforms (< 15 min)
   - 🏢 **Enterprise** - Compliance, scale (< 30 min)

## 4-Phase Methodology

BMad guides development through:
1. **📊 Analysis** - Brainstorm and explore solutions
2. **📝 Planning** - Create PRDs and technical specs
3. **🏗️ Solutioning** - Design architecture and UX
4. **⚡ Implementation** - Story-driven development with validation

## Core Agents (12 Specialized Roles)

**Development:** Developer, UX Designer, Tech Writer
**Architecture:** Architect, Test Architect, Game Architect
**Product:** PM, Analyst, Game Designer
**Leadership:** Scrum Master, BMad Master, Game Developer

Each agent brings domain expertise and supports customizable personalities.

## Core Modules

- **BMad Method (BMM)** - Complete agile framework with 34 workflows
- **BMad Builder (BMB)** - Create custom agents and domain-specific solutions
- **Creative Intelligence Suite (CIS)** - 5 creative facilitation workflows

## Key Features

- 🎨 Customizable agent personalities and expertise
- 🌐 Multi-language support (communication vs. code)
- 📄 Document sharding for 90% token savings
- 🔄 Update-safe customization persistence
- 🚀 Web bundles for ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini integration

## Configuration & Customization

Users can modify agent personalities, expertise levels, and communication styles through configuration files that persist across updates. Settings support separate language preferences for agent communication versus code generation.

## What's New in v6

**Major upgrades from v4:**
- Modular BMad Core framework architecture
- Scale-adaptive intelligence system
- Visual workflow SVG diagrams
- BMad Builder for custom solutions
- 50+ workflows (vs. 20 in v4)
- 19 specialized agents with enhanced customization
- Multi-language and document sharding support

## Documentation & Resources

- Quick Start Guide and complete BMM documentation available
- Agent customization guides included
- v4 to v6 upgrade guide provided
- Video tutorials on YouTube (@BMadCode)
- Web bundles at official repository

## Community & Support

- **Discord** - Community collaboration channel
- **GitHub Issues** - Bug reports and feature requests
- **YouTube** - Video tutorials and demonstrations
- Code of Conduct available in repository

## Development

Contributors can run:
```bash
npm test          # Run quality checks
npm run lint:fix  # Fix code style
npm run format:fix # Auto-format
npm run bundle    # Build web bundles
```

## License & Trademarks

MIT License. BMad™ and BMAD-METHOD™ are trademarks of BMad Code, LLC. Supported by DigitalOcean.

**v6 Status:** Near-beta quality alpha with improved stability and finalized documentation coming soon.

content.content:
# Agent Customization Guide Summary

## File Location & Structure

Customization files are located in `_bmad/_config/agents/` with the naming pattern `[agent-name].customize.yaml`. Each installed agent has its own customization file.

## Core Customizable Sections

### 1. **Agent Metadata**
You can modify how the agent identifies itself:
```yaml
agent:
  metadata:
    name: 'Custom Name'
```

### 2. **Persona**
Replace the agent's entire personality profile (not merged with defaults):
```yaml
persona:
  role: 'Your Role Title'
  identity: 'Description of identity'
  communication_style: 'Communication approach'
  principles:
    - 'Core principle one'
    - 'Core principle two'
```

### 3. **Memories**
Add persistent context the agent retains:
```yaml
memories:
  - 'Context statement one'
  - 'Context statement two'
```

### 4. **Menu Items**
Extend agent capabilities with custom workflows or actions:
```yaml
menu:
  - trigger: workflow-name
    workflow: '{project-root}/path/to/workflow.yaml'
    description: 'What this does'
```

### 5. **Critical Actions**
Define pre-execution instructions:
```yaml
critical_actions:
  - 'Instruction one'
  - 'Instruction two'
```

### 6. **Custom Prompts**
Create reusable prompts referenced as `action: '#prompt-id'`:
```yaml
prompts:
  - id: prompt-identifier
    content: |
      Multi-line prompt content here
```

## Essential Workflow

After editing a customization file, you must rebuild: `npx bmad-method@alpha build <agent-name>`

All customizations persist through framework updates and are project-specific rather than global.

content.content:
# Workflow Guide Summary

Based on the GitHub documentation page, here's what's available regarding workflows:

## Key Concepts

The page addresses three distinct approaches to workflow management:

**Workflow Vendoring** (currently supported)
- Enables accessing workflows from other modules without installing the entire module
- "Workflow Vendoring allows an agent to have access to a workflow from another module, without having to install said module."
- At installation, the vendored workflow and its dependencies are cloned into your module's location
- Implementation uses `exec-vendor` in agent configuration pointing to the source workflow path

**Workflow Inheritance** (post-beta support coming)
- Allows modification and extension of existing workflows
- Party Mode is cited as an example designed with inheritance capabilities
- Potential use cases include retrospectives, sprint planning, and brainstorming sessions

**Workflow Customization** (post-beta support coming)
- Enables user customization of specific workflows through hooks and configurable inputs
- "workflows with optional hooks, configurable inputs, and the ability to replace whole at install time"
- Allows step-level replacements while maintaining naming conventions and flow continuity

## Note

The document doesn't provide comprehensive structural details about workflow phases or complete creation guidance—those appear covered in other documentation files referenced in the repository's docs folder.

content.content:
# BMAD Custom Content Installation Guide

## Overview

The BMAD-METHOD repository provides a framework for creating and installing custom content extensions. This guide explains the process for installing agents, workflows, modules, and other custom components into a BMAD project.

## Content Types Supported

BMAD Core accommodates several categories of custom additions:

- Custom Standalone Modules
- Custom Add-On Modules
- Custom Global Modules
- Custom Agents
- Custom Workflows

## Creating Installable Custom Modules

### Required Structure

To make content installable, follow this approach:

1. **Folder Organization**: Create a folder with an abbreviated name (like `cis` for Creative Intelligence Suite). This folder name becomes your module identifier.

2. **Essential File**: Include a `module.yaml` file at the root level. This file drives the configuration questions presented during installation.

3. **Directory Layout**:
```
module-code/
├── module.yaml
├── agents/
├── workflows/
├── tools/
└── templates/
```

The `agents/` and `workflows/` folders enable automatic discovery of these components.

## Standalone Content Approach

For unrelated individual items, use this method:

1. Add `unitary: true` property in your `module.yaml`
2. Organize items in type-specific folders: `agents/`, `workflows/`, `templates/`, `tools/`, `tasks/`, `prompts/`
3. Each item gets its own subfolder

**Key distinction**: Items in unitary modules function independently and don't rely on each other, though they may reference workflows from other modules through vendoring.

## Installation Methods

### New Projects

During initial setup, the installer prompts: "Would you like to install a local custom module?" Select 'y' and provide the path to your folder containing `module.yaml`.

### Existing Projects

1. Run the installer targeting your project
2. Choose "Modify BMAD Installation"
3. Select the option to add or update custom modules

## Critical Naming Requirements

Avoid these common pitfalls:

- **Unique codes**: Don't use reserved codes like `bmm` (BMad Method)
- **No duplicates**: Each custom module needs distinct identification
- **Prevent collisions**: Ensure custom module names don't conflict with existing modules

Example mistakes to avoid include creating modules with codes matching official BMAD modules or repeating codes across multiple custom modules.

## Best Practices

- Use descriptive, unique module codes
- Document dependencies clearly
- Test modules in isolation
- Consider versioning systems for tracking updates

## Upcoming Features

Future enhancements will include selective installation for unitary modules (choose specific items) and automatic dependency resolution for add-on modules.

# BMAD Documentation Index Overview

## Getting Started Entry Points

The documentation provides three primary onboarding paths:

1. **Brand New to BMAD**: "Quick Start Guide" → "BMM Workflows Guide"
2. **v4 Upgrading Users**: "v4 to v6 Upgrade Guide" → "Quick Start Guide"
3. **Brownfield Projects**: "Brownfield Guide" → "Quick Start Guide"

## Core Documentation Structure

**Project-Level Resources:**
- README.md (feature summary, module introductions)
- CONTRIBUTING.md (guidelines, code style)
- CHANGELOG.md (version history, breaking changes)

**Installation & Setup:**
- v4 to v6 Upgrade Guide
- Document Sharding Guide (token optimization)
- Bundle Distribution Setup (maintainer guide)

## Module Documentation

**BMad Method (BMM)** – Main development framework including:
- Quick Start Guide
- Quick Spec Flow (rapid Level 0-1 development)
- Scale Adaptive System (5-level framework)
- Brownfield Guide
- Workflows Guide (marked as "ESSENTIAL READING")
- Test Architect Guide

**BMad Builder (BMB)** – Custom solution creation:
- Module Overview
- Agent Creation Guide

**Creative Intelligence Suite (CIS)** – Creative workflows:
- Module README

## Advanced Topics

- Custom Content Installation (agents, workflows, modules)
- Agent Customization Guide

## Six Recommended Reading Paths

The documentation outlines learning progressions for different user scenarios: software projects, game development, legacy system upgrades, brownfield work, custom solutions, and community contributions.

content.content:
# BMad Method V6 Quick Start Guide Summary

## Installation & Setup
Install via: `npx bmad-method@alpha install`. This creates a `_bmad/` folder with agents and workflows.

## Core Workflow Phases

**Phase 1 (Optional):** Analysis with Analyst agent
- Brainstorming, research, product briefs

**Phase 2 (Required):** Planning
- Quick Flow track: tech-spec only
- BMad Method/Enterprise: PRD + optional UX design

**Phase 3 (Track-dependent):** Solutioning
- BMad Method/Enterprise: Architecture design
- Followed by epics/stories creation

**Phase 4 (Required):** Implementation
- Sprint planning, story creation, development, code review

## Key Agents & Workflows

| Agent | Responsibilities |
|-------|------------------|
| **Analyst** | Initializes workflows, tracks progress |
| **PM** | Creates PRD, tech-specs, epics/stories |
| **UX-Designer** | UX/UI specifications (if needed) |
| **Architect** | System architecture, readiness checks |
| **SM (Scrum Master)** | Sprint planning, story management |
| **DEV** | Code implementation and reviews |

## Critical Best Practices

**Use fresh chats for each workflow** to avoid context limitations and hallucinations. Load the agent, wait for the menu, then request the workflow using natural language, shortcuts (e.g., `*prd`), or menu numbers.

## Tracking Files
- `bmm-workflow-status.yaml` — tracks planning phase progress
- `sprint-status.yaml` — manages epics and stories during implementation

## Getting Help
Query workflow status anytime by loading any agent and requesting "workflow-status" to see next steps. Community support available via Discord.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about **BMAD-METHOD v6** and its agents, workflows, and structure on GitHub:

## Overview

BMAD-Method v6 is a multi-agent framework that transforms chaotic AI interactions into systematic, spec-driven development workflows. It's like having a full software team - PM, Architect, DEV, QA - but they're all AI agents.

## Agent Structure

BMAD leverages specialized AI agents, each embodying a specific role within the software development lifecycle: Analyst, Product Manager, Architect, Scrum Master, Product Owner, Developer, and QA.(no content)The v6 release includes 8 BMM agents and team-fullstack web bundles (~25k lines). The agents are: analyst, architect, dev, pm, sm, tea, tech-writer, ux-designer, plus a team-fullstack bundle (complete BMAD Method team bundle).(no content)V6 has 50+ workflows (up from 20) and 19+ specialized agents (up from 12), along with new concepts like step-file architecture, document sharding, and web bundles.

## Workflow Structure

A BMAD workflow is a structured, YAML-based blueprint that orchestrates a series of tasks for different AI agents to complete. It acts as a project plan, guiding the AI from the initial concept to the final, completed project by defining a specific sequence of steps.(no content)In a greenfield-fullstack workflow, the process looks like this: The analyst agent creates a project brief. The pm agent takes that brief as a dependency to create a product requirements document (PRD). The architect agent then uses the PRD to design the system's architecture. This structured approach ensures that every step is purposeful.

## Development Phases

BMad Method guides you through a proven development lifecycle: Analysis (Optional) - Brainstorm, research, and explore solutions; Planning - Create PRDs, tech specs, or game design documents.(no content)Phase 3: Solutioning (Track-dependent) - Design the architecture for BMad Method and Enterprise tracks; Phase 4: Implementation (Required) - Build your software Epic by Epic, Story by Story.

## Project Structure

All modules install to a single bmad/ folder in your project with the structure: core/ (Core framework - always installed), bmm/ (BMad Method if selected), bmb/ (BMad Builder if selected), cis/ (Creative Intelligence Suite - shared resources), and _cfg/ (Your customizations including agent customization files).

## Key V6 Features

v6 is a MASSIVE upgrade from v4! Complete architectural overhaul, scale-adaptive intelligence, visual workflows, and the powerful BMad Core framework.(no content)v6 represents a complete architectural revolution from v4: BMad Core Framework - Modular architecture enabling custom domain solutions; Scale-Adaptive Intelligence - Automatic adjustment from bug fixes to enterprise; Visual Workflows - Beautiful SVG diagrams showing complete methodology.

## Installation

You can install using: `npx bmad-method@alpha install` for v6 Alpha (recommended) or `npx bmad-method install` for stable v4 for production.

The official GitHub repository is at: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

- Instala em uma pasta `_bmad/` (ou `bmad/`)
- Tem 4 fases: Analysis, Planning, Solutioning, Implementation
- Usa agents especializados: PM, Analyst, Architect, SM, DEV, TEA, UX-Designer, Tech-Writer
- Usa arquivos YAML para configuração
- Tem workflow-status.yaml para tracking
- Suporta customização via arquivos .customize.yaml

A estrutura que preciso criar:
```

├── bmm/              # BMad Method module
